The first codification task is mapping — building a cross-reference index that connects every provision to every other provision it relates to. This is exactly the kind of pattern-matching work where AI outperforms human effort.

Sample Prompt — Cross-Reference Mapping:

``` You are a legal codification specialist for the Bangsamoro Parliament.

I am uploading the full text of [NUMBER] enacted Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts. Build a cross-reference index as follows:

For each BAA, identify every section that: 1. Explicitly amends or repeals a provision in another BAA (cite both the amending and amended provisions) 2. Creates a mandate, power, or function that overlaps with a mandate in another BAA (cite both provisions and describe the overlap) 3. Defines a term that is also defined differently in another BAA (cite both definitions and flag the inconsistency) 4. References another BAA by number or subject matter

Output format: a table with columns: BAA (Source) | Section | BAA (Target) | Section | Relationship Type (amends, repeals, overlaps, defines differently, references)

Sort by BAA number. Flag all definitional inconsistencies as [TERMINOLOGY CONFLICT]. ```

Identifying Conflicts

Not all overlaps are conflicts. BAA 82 (Labor Code) and BAA 13 (Administrative Code) both address employment conditions, but they operate in different domains — one covers the private sector, the other structures government. A conflict exists when two provisions impose contradictory requirements on the same actor in the same situation.

Sample Prompt — Conflict Detection:

``` From the cross-reference index, extract all entries marked as “overlaps” or “defines differently.”

For each entry, assess: 1. Do the two provisions impose contradictory obligations on the same entity? 2. Could a person or office attempting to comply with both provisions face an impossible or ambiguous situation? 3. Does one provision appear to supersede the other (enacted later, more specific, or explicitly intended to prevail)?

Classify each overlap as: - CONFLICT (contradictory obligations, no clear resolution) - AMBIGUITY (unclear which provision controls) - COMPLEMENTARY (overlapping but compatible) - SUPERSEDED (one clearly prevails over the other)

For each CONFLICT or AMBIGUITY, recommend a resolution approach: amendment, IRR clarification, or parliamentary interpretation. ```

Terminology Consistency

One of the most common sources of legal ambiguity is inconsistent terminology. The same concept may be called different names in different BAAs enacted years apart. A ministry renamed through reorganization may still carry its old name in earlier enactments. A technical term defined in one code may be used without definition in another.

Sample Prompt — Terminology Audit:

``` Conduct a terminology audit across all uploaded BAAs.

  1. Extract every defined term from each BAA’s “Definition of Terms” section
  2. Identify terms that appear in multiple BAAs — compare definitions for consistency
  3. Identify terms used in BAA provisions that are NOT defined in that BAA but ARE defined in another BAA
  4. Identify instances where different BAAs use different terms for what appears to be the same concept (e.g., “barangay health station” vs. “barangay health center” vs. “community health facility”)
  5. Flag all inconsistencies as [DEFINE], [RECONCILE], or [STANDARDIZE]

Output: a terminology concordance table sorted alphabetically, with each term showing all BAAs where it appears and how it is defined or used. ```

Building the Bangsamoro Legal Corpus Database

The long-term codification goal is a searchable legal corpus — a structured database where every provision is tagged by subject, ministry, date of enactment, amendment history, and related provisions. AI can generate the initial structure.

Practical steps for building the corpus:

  1. Digitize all enacted BAAs in a consistent format (markdown or structured text)
  2. Tag every provision with metadata: BAA number, section number, subject category, responsible ministry, effective date
  3. Map all amendments — when BAA 37 amends BAA 13, Section 9, link them bidirectionally
  4. Generate subject-matter clusters — all provisions related to health, all provisions related to procurement, all provisions related to local government
  5. Build a change log — when a new BAA amends or repeals an existing provision, the corpus updates automatically

This connects directly to Chapter 4 (Data, Documents, and Knowledge Management), where building BARMM-specific AI knowledge bases is covered in detail.

11.3 AI for IRR Development

Why IRRs Matter

An enacted BAA sets the legal framework. The Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) translate that framework into operational detail. The BAA says what must be done. The IRR specifies how, by whom, within what timeline, using what forms, following what procedures.

Without an IRR, an enacted BAA often remains partially or fully unimplemented. This is not a theoretical problem in BARMM. Chapter 10 documented the compliance tracking challenge: some priority codes have been enacted for years without complete IRRs.[^5] The delay is not always about political will. Drafting an IRR requires deep legal analysis, operational expertise, and interagency coordination — resources that implementing agencies may lack.

AI does not replace the implementing agency’s operational knowledge. But it generates the structural framework of an IRR from the enacted BAA text, which the agency then fills in with operational specifics.

Extracting IRR Requirements from Enacted BAAs

Before drafting an IRR, you need to know exactly what the BAA requires.

Sample Prompt — IRR Requirement Extraction:

``` You are a legal analyst supporting IRR development for the Bangsamoro Government.

I am uploading [BAA NUMBER AND TITLE]. Extract every provision that requires implementing rules, regulations, guidelines, or operational procedures.

For each provision: 1. Section number and title 2. The specific obligation (“shall issue implementing rules,” “as may be prescribed by rules,” “in accordance with guidelines to be promulgated,” etc.) 3. The implementing body (which ministry, office, or commission is responsible) 4. The deadline (if specified in the BAA) 5. The scope — what operational detail the IRR must supply

Also identify provisions that do NOT explicitly require an IRR but that are too general to implement without additional operational guidance. Flag these as [RECOMMENDED IRR PROVISION].

Output: a table sorted by section number. ```

Drafting IRR Frameworks

Once requirements are extracted, AI can generate an IRR framework — the structural skeleton that the implementing agency completes.

Sample Prompt — IRR Framework Draft:

``` Draft an IRR framework for [BAA NUMBER AND TITLE].

Structure the IRR as follows: RULE I — Title and Authority RULE II — Definition of Terms (carry over definitions from the BAA; add operational definitions needed for implementation) RULE III — Coverage and Applicability RULE IV onward — One rule per major substantive provision of the BAA that requires operational detail FINAL RULE — Effectivity and Repealing Clause

For each rule: 1. Cite the specific BAA section being implemented 2. State the implementing agency’s obligation 3. Identify the operational details the IRR must specify (forms, timelines, procedures, responsible offices) 4. Leave placeholders marked [AGENCY TO SUPPLY] where operational specifics depend on the agency’s internal structure and resources

Include a compliance timeline: which rules take effect immediately upon promulgation and which have phased implementation dates.

[Upload the full BAA text] ```

The IRR Quality Checklist

Use AI to verify that a draft IRR meets quality standards before promulgation.

Table 11.2: IRR Quality Verification Checklist

Check What AI Verifies What Humans Verify
Completeness Every BAA provision requiring an IRR has a corresponding rule Whether the operational detail is sufficient for field implementation
Legal fidelity IRR rules do not exceed, contradict, or narrow the authority granted by the BAA Whether the interpretation of BAA provisions reflects legislative intent
Definitional consistency Terms defined in the IRR match the BAA’s definitions; no undefined terms Whether operational definitions align with agency practice
Timeline feasibility Compliance deadlines are internally consistent Whether the agency can realistically meet the deadlines
Interagency clarity Where multiple agencies share implementation responsibility, roles are clearly delineated Whether the agencies have agreed to the delineation
BOL alignment IRR provisions fall within the Bangsamoro Government’s powers under BOL Article V, Section 2 Whether the IRR triggers intergovernmental coordination requirements under BOL Article VI

Case Study: Drafting the IRR Framework for BAA 84 (Bangsamoro Budget System Act)

BAA 84 establishes the Bangsamoro budget system framework, covering the entire budget cycle from preparation through accountability.[^6] The Budget System Act is one of the most operationally complex BAAs in the Bangsamoro corpus because it touches every ministry, every office, and every local government unit within BARMM.

An AI-assisted IRR workflow for BAA 84 would proceed as follows:

Step 1 — Extract. Upload the full text of BAA 84. Prompt the AI to extract every provision requiring implementing rules. The extraction should identify provisions on budget preparation calendars, submission formats, allotment release procedures, obligation authority limits, accountability reporting requirements, and the roles of the Ministry of Finance, Budget, and Management (MFBM) in each stage.

Step 2 — Map dependencies. BAA 84 does not operate in isolation. It interacts with annual GAAs (BAA 85 for FY 2026), the Administrative Code (BAA 13), and the Civil Service Code (BAA 17) for personnel budgeting. Prompt the AI to identify all cross-references between BAA 84 and other enacted BAAs. Where BAA 84 creates obligations that depend on provisions in other BAAs, the IRR must address the coordination mechanism.

Step 3 — Draft. Generate the IRR framework with one rule per major budget cycle stage. Leave operational placeholders for MFBM to supply: specific forms, submission deadlines for the next fiscal year, allotment release schedules, and accountability report templates.

Step 4 — Verify. Run the draft IRR through the quality checklist (Table 11.2). Confirm that every BAA 84 provision has a corresponding rule. Check that definitions match. Verify that the IRR does not create obligations beyond what BAA 84 authorizes.

What the AI cannot do: Determine whether MFBM has the staff capacity to implement a quarterly budget review cycle or whether a monthly allotment release schedule is operationally feasible. Those judgments require the implementing agency’s institutional knowledge.

11.4 AI for Resolution Drafting and Parliamentary Operations

Resolutions vs. Bills

The Bangsamoro Parliament produces two main types of legislative measures: bills (which become BAAs when enacted) and resolutions (which express the sense of Parliament without the force of law, or which address internal parliamentary matters).[^7] Chapter 7 covered AI for bill drafting. This section addresses the distinct requirements of resolution drafting and the parliamentary operations that surround it.

Resolutions serve several purposes in the Bangsamoro Parliament:

  • Congratulatory resolutions — recognizing achievements of individuals, organizations, or communities
  • Commendatory resolutions — commending specific actions or milestones
  • Commemorative resolutions — marking historical events, holidays, or occasions
  • Policy resolutions — expressing Parliament’s position on a policy issue without enacting binding law
  • Oversight resolutions — directing committees to investigate or report on specific matters
  • Internal resolutions — governing parliamentary procedures, committee creation, or rules amendments

The WHEREAS-RESOLVED Structure

Every resolution follows a standard two-part structure: preamble clauses (WHEREAS) that establish the factual and legal basis, followed by operative clauses (RESOLVED) that state what Parliament is actually doing.[^8]

AI can generate both components from a brief description of the resolution’s purpose.

Sample Prompt — Resolution Draft:

``` You are a legislative drafter for the Bangsamoro Parliament.

Draft a [TYPE] resolution for the following purpose: [DESCRIBE THE PURPOSE IN 2-3 SENTENCES]

Structure: 1. TITLE: “A Resolution [verb]-ing [subject]” 2. WHEREAS CLAUSES (preamble): - Clause 1: Legal basis (cite the BOL provision, BAA, or Constitutional provision that gives Parliament authority) - Clause 2: Factual background (what happened or what condition exists) - Clause 3-5: Supporting facts, prior legislative action, or policy context - Each WHEREAS clause should be one sentence - Begin with “WHEREAS,” followed by the statement, ending with a semicolon 3. RESOLVED CLAUSES (operative): - “NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, as it is hereby resolved,” followed by the operative provision - If multiple operative clauses: “RESOLVED, FURTHER,” - Final clause: effectivity

Use formal legislative language. Cite specific BAA sections and BOL articles where applicable.

[Provide any supporting documents or factual details] ```

Strengthening WHEREAS Clauses

Weak preamble clauses undermine even well-intentioned resolutions. A WHEREAS clause that says “WHEREAS, the Bangsamoro Parliament recognizes the importance of education” adds nothing. A strong WHEREAS clause cites a specific provision, references specific data, or states a specific fact.

Sample Prompt — WHEREAS Clause Strengthening:

``` Review the following draft resolution for the Bangsamoro Parliament. Strengthen each WHEREAS clause by:

  1. Adding specific legal citations (BOL article and section, BAA number and section, or national law)
  2. Replacing general statements with specific facts, data points, or documented events
  3. Ensuring each WHEREAS clause builds logically on the previous one (legal basis → factual background → specific circumstances → justification for action)
  4. Removing redundant clauses that repeat the same point in different words

For any factual claim you cannot verify from the uploaded documents, mark it as [VERIFY: source needed].

[Paste the draft resolution] ```

Parliamentary Operations Support

Beyond individual resolutions, AI assists with the operational machinery of Parliament:

Table 11.3: Parliamentary Operations with AI Support

Operation Manual Process AI-Assisted Process
Order of Business preparation Secretary manually compiles agenda items from committee reports and pending measures AI generates draft order of business from committee status reports, pending bills, and calendar deadlines
Committee referral Speaker’s office determines appropriate committee(s) based on subject matter AI recommends primary and secondary committee referrals by matching bill subject against committee jurisdictions
Co-authorship tracking Staff manually tracks which MPs have signed onto each measure AI maintains a co-authorship database and generates reports on sponsorship patterns
Session minutes synthesis Staff transcribes and summarizes plenary proceedings AI converts session recordings or transcripts into structured minutes with speaker attribution
Legislative calendar management Manual tracking of filing deadlines, committee report deadlines, and plenary scheduling AI generates timeline alerts and identifies scheduling conflicts

11.5 AI for Legislative Tracking and Status Reporting

The Tracking Challenge

At any given time, the Bangsamoro Parliament has dozens of measures at different stages: bills filed but not yet referred, bills in committee, bills reported out for plenary debate, bills pending interpellation, resolutions awaiting adoption. Staff, committee chairs, and Members of Parliament all need to know the status of specific measures — and no one has time to compile the answer manually from scattered records.

AI builds and maintains the legislative tracking system that makes status reporting instantaneous.

Building the Legislative Status Database

The foundation is a structured database that captures every measure and its current status.

Sample Prompt — Legislative Status Database Design:

``` Design a legislative tracking database structure for the Bangsamoro Parliament.

Fields for each measure: 1. Measure type (bill or resolution) 2. Measure number (e.g., PB-424, PR-47) 3. Short title 4. Principal author(s) 5. Co-author(s) 6. Date filed 7. Committee referral (primary and secondary committees) 8. Date referred to committee 9. Committee action (hearing scheduled, hearing completed, committee report filed, substitute bill proposed) 10. Date of committee report 11. Plenary status (first reading, second reading, interpellation, period of amendments, third reading, enrolled) 12. Date of plenary action 13. For bills: BAA number assigned upon enactment 14. Related measures (companion bills, substitute bills, amendments to existing BAAs)

Include status categories: FILED → REFERRED → IN COMMITTEE → REPORTED OUT → PLENARY (1ST READING → 2ND READING → 3RD READING) → ENROLLED → ENACTED

Generate sample entries using the following real measures: - PB-424 (latest filed bill) - BAA 86 (Parliamentary District Act, enacted January 2026) - BAA 84 (Budget System Act) ```

Generating Status Reports

Once the database exists, AI generates status reports on demand.

Sample Prompt — Legislative Pipeline Report:

``` From the legislative tracking database, generate a status report covering:

  1. PIPELINE SUMMARY: Total measures filed, in committee, reported out, enacted — broken down by type (bill vs. resolution)
  2. COMMITTEE WORKLOAD: For each standing committee, list the number of measures pending, measures acted on this quarter, and average days from referral to committee report
  3. AGING REPORT: List all measures that have been in committee for more than [N] days without action
  4. ENACTMENT RATE: Percentage of filed bills that reached enactment, by session period
  5. PRIORITY LEGISLATION STATUS: Status of any measures identified as priority by the Chief Minister or the Speaker

Format: executive summary (1 paragraph) followed by detailed tables. Flag all measures older than 180 days in committee as [REVIEW NEEDED]. ```

Committee Referral Recommendations

When a new bill is filed, it must be referred to the appropriate committee. Some bills clearly belong to a single committee. Others involve multiple policy domains and require referral to a primary committee with secondary referral to other committees.

Sample Prompt — Committee Referral Analysis:

``` A new bill has been filed in the Bangsamoro Parliament:

Title: [BILL TITLE] Subject: [BRIEF DESCRIPTION]

Based on the following committee jurisdictions of the Bangsamoro Parliament, recommend: 1. PRIMARY COMMITTEE: The committee with principal jurisdiction over the bill’s primary subject matter 2. SECONDARY COMMITTEE(S): Any committees with overlapping jurisdiction that should review specific provisions 3. JUSTIFICATION: For each recommendation, cite the specific provisions of the bill that fall within that committee’s jurisdiction

If the bill involves budgetary implications, always include the Committee on Appropriations as a secondary referral.

[Paste the bill title, explanatory note, or full text] [Paste the committee jurisdiction list] ```

Tracking Amendments to Existing BAAs

Some of the 89 BAAs have already been amended. BAA 3 was amended by BAA 14. BAA 13 was amended by BAA 37. BAA 23 was amended by BAA 33. BAA 32 was amended by BAA 52. BAA 35 was amended by BAA 87 and BAA 88. BAA 54 was amended by BAA 70. BAA 55 was amended by BAA 71. BAA 58 was superseded by BAA 86 after the Supreme Court struck down both BAA 58 and BAA 77.[^9]

Tracking these amendment chains manually is error-prone. AI maintains the amendment history.

Sample Prompt — Amendment Chain Tracker:

``` From the following list of 89 BAAs, identify every amendment relationship.

For each amendment: 1. Amending BAA (number and title) 2. Amended BAA (number and title) 3. Specific section(s) amended 4. Nature of amendment (modifies text, extends deadline, repeals provision, adds new provision) 5. Whether the amendment is the most recent change to that section (or whether it has been further amended)

Also identify: - BAAs that have been struck down by the Supreme Court (cite the case) - BAAs that repeal provisions of predecessor laws (e.g., MMAA provisions repealed by BAAs)

Output: amendment chain table sorted by original BAA number, showing the full amendment history for each.

[Paste or upload the BAA index with amendment notes] ```

11.6 Closing the Cycle: How Enacted Legislation Becomes Planning Input

The Legislation-Planning Loop

The governance cycle in BARMM does not end with enactment. Every enacted BAA creates new conditions that the planning process must account for. The Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028 identifies six overarching goals and eight cross-cutting strategies.[^10] Each enacted BAA either advances those goals or creates new implementation requirements that the next planning cycle must address.

This feedback loop is the most neglected stage in most governments. Laws are enacted, and the planning agencies continue working from their original assumptions as if nothing changed. AI can close this gap by systematically extracting the planning implications of every new enactment.

From Enacted BAA to Planning Input

Sample Prompt — Legislative Impact on Planning:

``` You are a strategic planning analyst for the Bangsamoro Development Authority (BPDA).

I am uploading [BAA NUMBER AND TITLE], recently enacted by the Bangsamoro Parliament. Analyze its implications for the Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028.

For each major provision: 1. Which BDP goal(s) does it advance? (cite the specific goal and strategy) 2. Does it create new implementation obligations that require planning resources (staffing, budget, infrastructure)? 3. Does it change the institutional landscape (new agencies, new mandates, new coordination requirements)? 4. Does it require adjustments to existing plans or targets? 5. Does it create new monitoring indicators that should be added to the BDP results framework?

Output: a planning implications table with columns: BAA Provision | BDP Goal Affected | Planning Implication | Action Required | Responsible Agency

Flag any provision that creates unfunded mandates as [BUDGET IMPLICATION - COORDINATE WITH MFBM]. ```

Mapping BAAs to BDP Goals

AI can build a comprehensive map connecting all 89 BAAs to their corresponding BDP goals. This map serves multiple purposes: it shows Parliament which development goals have strong legislative support and which have gaps. It shows the planning agencies which laws they must account for. And it provides the evidence base for the next planning cycle.

Table 11.4: Sample BAA-to-BDP Goal Mapping

BDP Goal BAAs That Advance This Goal Coverage Assessment
Goal 1: Just and lasting peace BAA 57 (Mujahideen Assistance), BAA 60-61 (Memorials), BAA 62 (IDP Protection), BAA 89 (Transitional Justice) Moderate — peace financing and transitional justice addressed; community-level reconciliation programs need legislative framework
Goal 2: Moral governance BAA 13 (Administrative Code), BAA 17 (Civil Service Code), BAA 84 (Budget System Act) Strong foundational codes — IRR implementation is the gap
Goal 3: Meaningful self-determination BAA 35, 86-88 (Electoral laws), BAA 49 (Local Governance Code), BAA 41-48, 53-55 (New municipalities) Strong — electoral and local governance framework largely in place
Goal 4: Economic development BAA 82 (Labor Code), BAA 9, 19, 59, 72 (Labor/employment measures) Revenue Code and Investment Code still needed
Goal 5: Quality social services BAA 18 (Education Code), BAA 25-30, 73-74, 76, 78-80 (Health), BAA 40 (Science HS), BAA 83 (Nutrition) Health infrastructure strong; social protection framework needs expansion
Goal 6: Cultural identity BAA 1, 2, 7, 16, 20 (Symbols), BAA 39 (Holidays), BAA 50 (Kulliyyah), BAA 64 (IP Rights) Cultural symbols established; cultural industry development legislation needed

Feeding Oversight Findings Back into Legislation

Chapter 10 traced the accountability chain from AI-generated oversight analysis to formal parliamentary action.[^11] The final link in that chain connects back to this chapter: when oversight reveals a gap in existing legislation, the finding becomes the basis for a new bill or amendment.

The complete governance cycle with AI support:

  1. Plan (Chapter 5) — BDP sets goals and strategies
  2. Budget (Chapter 6) — GAA allocates resources to achieve goals
  3. Legislate (Chapter 7) — Parliament enacts BAAs creating the legal framework
  4. Implement (Chapter 8) — Ministries execute through IRRs and programs
  5. Monitor (Chapter 9) — M&E tracks progress against targets
  6. Oversee (Chapter 10) — Parliament holds the executive accountable
  7. Codify (Chapter 11) — Enacted legislation is organized, harmonized, and fed back into planning

AI supports every stage. The output of each stage becomes the input for the next. The Bangsamoro legal corpus is not a static archive — it is a living system that grows, adapts, and informs the continuous cycle of governance.

Cross-References to Other Chapters

Topic Where to Go
Building the BARMM legal knowledge base Chapter 4, Section 4.3
AI for strategic planning with BDP alignment Chapter 5, Section 5.2
Budget legislation and GAA analysis Chapter 6, Sections 6.2-6.4
AI for bill drafting (detailed process) Chapter 7, Sections 7.2-7.5
Oversight findings that trigger new legislation Chapter 10, Section 10.6
Ethics of AI in legal codification Chapter 12, Section 12.3
Prompt library for codification tasks Appendix A, Section A.6

Summary

The Bangsamoro Parliament’s 89 BAAs are a legislative achievement. Transforming them into a coherent, searchable, internally consistent legal system is the next institutional challenge. Codification, harmonization, IRR development, parliamentary operations management, legislative tracking, and the legislation-planning feedback loop — these are the tasks that determine whether enacted laws translate into functioning institutions.

AI does not codify. It does not harmonize conflicting provisions based on policy intent. It does not decide which IRR interpretation best serves legislative purpose. It does not exercise the Speaker’s judgment on committee referrals. Those are human functions.

But AI identifies every cross-reference across 89 BAAs in minutes. It flags every terminology inconsistency. It generates IRR frameworks that agencies can refine rather than build from nothing. It tracks amendment chains that no staff member can hold in memory. It builds the legislative tracking system that makes pipeline management instantaneous. And it maps enacted legislation back to development goals so the planning cycle stays current.

The practical workflow follows the same pattern as every chapter in this guidebook:

  1. Prepare the corpus — digitize and tag all enacted BAAs, resolutions, and IRRs
  2. Prompt precisely — use the structured prompts in this chapter, adapted to your specific codification or tracking task
  3. Verify against enacted text — AI-generated cross-references, conflict flags, and IRR frameworks must be checked against the actual provisions
  4. Connect to action — every codification finding should lead to a concrete outcome: an amendment proposal, an IRR deadline, a planning adjustment, or a harmonization recommendation

The final chapters of this guidebook address the ethical framework (Chapter 12), the implementation roadmap (Chapter 13), training and capacity building (Chapter 14), and the long-term vision for AI-augmented governance in BARMM (Chapter 15).

Footnotes

[^1]: The Bangsamoro Transition Authority Parliament has enacted BAA 1 through BAA 89 as of January 2026. See the BAA Quick-Reference Index for the complete categorized list.

[^2]: The 13 domain categories are derived from the BAA Quick-Reference Index: Governance & Administration, Budget & Appropriations, Health, Education & Culture, Economy & Labor, Environment & Development, Social Welfare & Human Rights, Electoral, Local Government, Symbols & Identity, Peace & Security, Holidays, and Miscellaneous.

[^3]: Republic Act No. 11054 (Bangsamoro Organic Law), Art. XVI, Sec. 4. The BOL mandates the BTA to enact priority codes including the Administrative Code, Revenue Code, Electoral Code, Local Government Code, Civil Service Code, Education Code, and Investment Code during the transition period.

[^4]: BAA 84 (Bangsamoro Budget System Act of 2025) establishes the comprehensive budget cycle framework for the Bangsamoro Government. BAA 82 (Bangsamoro Labor and Employment Code) provides the labor and employment regulatory framework for BARMM.

[^5]: See Chapter 10, Section 10.4 (Compliance Monitoring Across BAAs) and the case study on tracking IRR compliance across priority codes.

[^6]: BAA 84 (Bangsamoro Budget System Act of 2025). The Budget System Act covers budget preparation, authorization, execution, and accountability for the Bangsamoro Government.

[^7]: Saidamen R. Mambayao, Bill Drafting Guidebook for the Bangsamoro Parliament: A Practical Guide for Members of Parliament, Parliamentary and Legislative Staff, Bill Drafters, and Consultants (2026), Chapters 2 and 5. The Bill Drafting Guidebook distinguishes between bills (which become BAAs) and resolutions (which express the sense of Parliament).

[^8]: The WHEREAS-RESOLVED structure follows standard parliamentary drafting convention. WHEREAS clauses establish the preamble (factual and legal basis); RESOLVED clauses state the operative provisions. See the Bill Drafting Guidebook, Chapter 5, for the complete resolution drafting framework.

[^9]: Amendment chain as of March 2026: BAA 14 amends BAA 3 (extends FY 2020 appropriations). BAA 37 amends BAA 13 (seat of government). BAA 33 amends BAA 23 (extends FY 2022 GAA). BAA 52 amends BAA 32 (extends FY 2023 GAA). BAA 87 and BAA 88 amend BAA 35 (Electoral Code). BAA 70 amends BAA 54 (Municipality of Datu Sinsuat Balabaran). BAA 71 amends BAA 55 (Municipality of Sheik Abas Hamza). BAA 77 and BAA 58 were declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court in Ali et al. v. BTA (G.R. No. E-02219) and Macapaar et al. v. COMELEC (G.R. No. E-02235), decided September 30, 2025. BAA 86 (Parliamentary District Act) was enacted in January 2026 as the replacement.

[^10]: Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 4. The BDP identifies six overarching development goals: (1) Just and lasting peace, (2) Moral governance, (3) Meaningful self-determination, (4) Economic development and poverty reduction, (5) Quality social services, (6) Cultural identity and diversity. Eight cross-cutting strategies support these goals.

[^11]: See Chapter 10, Section 10.6 (The Accountability Chain: From AI Analysis to Formal Action). The six-link accountability chain traces the path from AI-generated detection through verification, preparation, committee action, follow-up, and formal recommendation.

Back to Table of Contents
12
Ethics, Islamic Values, and Responsible AI

Chapter 12 — Ethics, Islamic Values, and Responsible AI

Every tool is morally neutral until a human picks it up. A spreadsheet can track disaster relief distributions or hide stolen funds. A policy brief can clarify public debate or manipulate it. A budget proposal can allocate resources justly or divert them to political allies. The tool does not decide. You do.

AI is no different. It can draft legislation that serves the public interest or generate plausible-sounding text that obscures accountability. It can analyze budget data to reveal inequities or produce analyses that confirm whatever conclusion you wanted. It can accelerate government services to underserved communities or widen the gap between connected insiders and everyone else.

The Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao has something that most AI governance frameworks worldwide lack: a moral foundation that predates the technology. The Twelve Pillars of Moral Governance, the Islamic principles embedded in the Bangsamoro Organic Law, and the Enhanced 12-Point Priority Agenda provide an ethical infrastructure that is not borrowed from Silicon Valley or imported from a UN working paper. It grows from the identity and values of the Bangsamoro people themselves.[^1]

This chapter connects that moral foundation to the practical reality of AI use in government. It does not offer abstract philosophy. It gives you specific principles, clear boundaries, and a Bangsamoro-grounded framework for deciding when AI serves public interest — and when it does not.

12.1 Moral Governance and AI

The BARMM government anchors its operations in Moral Governance — a leadership framework built on twelve pillars that guide all public servants in the Bangsamoro region.[^2] These pillars are not aspirational slogans. They are operational expectations. Every ministry, office, and agency is expected to reflect them in daily decisions.

When you introduce AI into government work, you do not suspend these pillars. You extend them to cover a new tool. The question is not whether AI aligns with Moral Governance. The question is how you use AI so that it aligns.

12.1.1 The 12 Pillars Applied to AI Adoption

Each pillar creates specific obligations for anyone using AI in government work. Table 12.1 maps all twelve to concrete AI governance applications.

Table 12.1: The 12 Pillars of Moral Governance Applied to AI

# Pillar Core Principle AI Governance Application
1 Integrity Public office holders must not bind themselves in any manner that could influence their duties Do not use AI tools that require surrendering government data to private companies without data protection agreements. Do not allow vendor lock-in that compromises independent judgment.
2 Accountability Public office holders must be open and transparent; must answer to authority Every AI-assisted document must identify that AI was used in its preparation. The signing official takes full responsibility for the output. Never attribute a decision to “the AI said so.”
3 Transparency and Honesty Declare private interests; make all public transactions public Disclose AI assistance in policy recommendations, legislative briefers, and budget analyses. If a ministry uses AI to draft a bill, the committee reviewing it has a right to know.
4 Sense of Patriotism Invest in the Bangsamoro way of life and heritage Use AI to strengthen — not replace — Bangsamoro cultural knowledge. Train AI with local context and Bangsamoro source materials, not generic global templates.
5 Striving for Excellence Deliver the highest quality, prioritizing process over results AI enables faster output. Do not use speed as an excuse to skip review, verification, and quality assurance. A fast but inaccurate briefer is worse than a slow but correct one.
6 Cultural Understanding and Tolerance Appreciate socioeconomic differences and provincial diversity Test AI outputs for cultural bias. Check whether AI-generated policy language accounts for differences between mainland and island provinces, between Islamized and non-Moro indigenous communities, and between urban and rural populations.
7 Unwavering Loyalty to God Public officials are guided by devotion to God AI is a tool, not an authority. It does not issue fatwas, interpret divine commands, or replace the moral compass that faith provides. Use it within the boundaries of your faith, not as a substitute for it.
8 Justice Serve without prejudice; equity triumphs over personal allegiance Audit AI outputs for discriminatory patterns. If an AI-assisted analysis systematically disadvantages a particular province, ethnic group, or gender, reject it. Justice requires that AI serves everyone equitably.
9 Inclusivity Foster environments free from discrimination Ensure AI adoption does not create a two-tier bureaucracy where tech-literate staff advance while others are left behind. Design training that reaches all staff, including those in island and remote provinces.
10 Objectivity Make only merit-based decisions in the public interest Use AI to reduce bias in analysis, not introduce it. When AI summarizes stakeholder input, verify that it represents all voices — not just the loudest or most digitally accessible ones.
11 Dialogue and Meaningful Engagement Seek community opinion through dialogue Consult staff, communities, and stakeholders before deploying AI in services that affect them. Do not automate processes that communities expect to involve human interaction without first discussing the change.
12 Balance Between Practical Life and Moral Side Balance practical duties with moral/ethical values AI maximizes efficiency. But efficiency without ethics produces harm. Before automating any process, ask: does this serve the community’s well-being, or just our workload?

The table is not decorative. Print it. Post it in your office. When you are about to use AI for a government task, scan the twelve pillars and ask yourself: does this use honor every one? If any pillar is compromised, stop and redesign the approach.

12.1.2 Transparency and Honesty in AI-Assisted Outputs

Pillar 3 — Transparency and Honesty — deserves its own section because it is the pillar most frequently violated in AI-assisted government work. The violation is rarely deliberate. It happens by omission.

The pattern works like this. A policy analyst uses AI to draft a legislative briefer. The briefer is well-structured, properly cited, and convincingly written. The analyst submits it to the committee chairperson. The chairperson reads it, approves it, and presents its findings in a committee hearing. At no point does anyone disclose that AI assisted in the drafting. The committee members, the ministry officials responding to the briefer’s findings, and the public watching the hearing all assume the analysis is entirely the product of the parliamentary staff’s expertise.

Is the briefer wrong? Not necessarily. But is the process honest? No.

The Transparency Rule for AI-Assisted Government Outputs:

  1. Label AI-assisted documents. Include a note — “This document was prepared with AI assistance. All content has been reviewed and verified by [name and designation].” Place it in the document header or at the end, before the signature block.
  2. Identify what AI contributed. Was it the initial draft? The research synthesis? The data analysis? The formatting? Be specific.
  3. Confirm human verification. State who reviewed the AI output, what verification steps were taken, and what sources were checked.
  4. Do not present AI speed as human productivity. If your team produced a 30-page analysis in two days that would normally take two weeks, acknowledge that AI was part of the process. Claiming the output as purely manual work misrepresents your team’s capacity and sets unrealistic expectations for future work.

This transparency rule applies to all outputs covered in this guidebook: legislative briefers (Chapter 7), budget analyses (Chapter 6), M&E reports (Chapter 9), oversight analyses (Chapter 10), and policy recommendations.

12.2 Islamic Principles for AI Use

The Bangsamoro Organic Law recognizes Islamic values as integral to governance in the region.[^3] Article X establishes Shari’ah jurisdiction, with the Qur’an and Sunnah as its principal sources.[^4] Article IX, Section 24 mandates the preservation and promotion of Bangsamoro culture and identity.[^5] The Wali serves as the ceremonial head representing the moral authority of the Bangsamoro people.[^6]

These provisions are not ceremonial. They establish that governance in BARMM operates within an Islamic moral framework. When a new tool enters that framework, it must be evaluated against the principles that govern it.

Four Islamic principles are directly relevant to AI adoption in government. Each creates a specific obligation.

12.2.1 Amanah — Trustworthiness

Amanah means trust — the trust placed in you by God, by your institution, and by the people you serve. In the supervision guidebook, amanah is defined as the ethical foundation of the supervisory role: “You hold a trust. Your staff trust you to be fair. Your superiors trust you to deliver results. The public trusts you to use resources wisely.”[^7]

Applied to AI, amanah creates three obligations:

First, protect data entrusted to you. When you upload government documents to an AI tool, you transfer information that the public entrusted to your office. Ask: Does this AI tool store the data? Who has access? Is the data processed on servers outside the Philippines? If a citizen’s personal information appears in the document, does the AI tool comply with the Data Privacy Act of 2012 (Republic Act No. 10173)?[^8]

Practical test: Before uploading any document to an AI tool, answer these questions:

  • Does the document contain personally identifiable information?
  • Does the document contain classified or restricted information?
  • Does the AI tool’s privacy policy allow data retention and model training on uploaded content?
  • Would you be comfortable if this document appeared publicly because of a data breach at the AI provider?

If any answer gives you pause, do not upload the document. Use the AI tool with sanitized or anonymized content instead.

Second, verify before you submit. Amanah requires that what you present is truthful. AI generates plausible text — but plausible is not the same as accurate. If you submit an AI-drafted briefer containing a fabricated BAA number, an incorrect provision reference, or an invented statistic, you have breached amanah. The AI did not lie. You failed to verify.

Third, do not overstate AI’s contribution as your own — or understate it. Both directions violate amanah. Claiming AI work as purely your own is dishonest. Dismissing your own analytical judgment as “just AI” understates the human expertise that shaped the prompt, selected the sources, and verified the output. Accurate representation is the standard.

“Every one of you is a shepherd, and every one of you will be asked about his flock.” — Hadith (Bukhari and Muslim)[^9]

The hadith applies directly. The document you sign is part of your flock. The data you handle is part of your flock. The AI tool you select and the outputs you submit — all of it is your responsibility.

12.2.2 Adl — Justice

Adl means justice and fairness. In Islamic governance, justice is not optional — it is obligatory. The Qur’an commands: “O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm in justice, witnesses for Allah, even if it be against yourselves or parents and relatives” (An-Nisa 4:135).[^10]

Applied to AI, adl requires you to ask: Does this AI application treat everyone fairly?

Bias in AI is real. Large language models are trained on internet text. That text reflects the biases of its authors — and most of that text was written in English by people in North America, Europe, and East Asia. When you use AI to analyze data about BARMM communities, the AI has almost no training data about Maguindanaon governance traditions, Tausug maritime culture, or Teduray highland communities.[^11] It will default to generalizations. Those generalizations may be wrong, and they may systematically disadvantage communities that are already underrepresented.

Justice requires active checking. Do not assume AI output is neutral. Examine it for:

Bias Type How It Appears in Government AI Use Justice Response
Geographic bias AI recommendations that favor mainland provinces over island provinces because more data exists for the mainland Deliberately include island-province data; flag recommendations that ignore Tawi-Tawi, Sulu, and Basilan
Linguistic bias AI outputs that default to English or Filipino framing, ignoring Bangsamoro-specific terminology Review for culturally appropriate language; ensure AI preserves terms like amanah, shura, maslahah
Gender bias AI analysis that underrepresents women’s participation in governance or economic activity Cross-check AI-generated demographic analysis against gender-disaggregated data
Ethnic bias AI recommendations that treat the Bangsamoro as a monolithic group, ignoring 13 ethnolinguistic communities Verify that policy analysis accounts for Maguindanaon, Meranao, Tausug, Sama, Yakan, Iranun, and non-Moro IP communities
Socioeconomic bias AI-assisted service delivery analysis that focuses on digitally connected populations Include data from communities without internet access; remember that the digital divide maps directly onto poverty

BARMM example: Imagine an AI-assisted analysis of disaster resilience across the region. The AI recommends concentrating emergency infrastructure investments in Cotabato City and Marawi because they have the highest population density and the most historical damage data. The analysis is technically defensible. But it ignores Tawi-Tawi’s vulnerability to typhoons and the island municipalities’ near-total lack of emergency infrastructure. Adl requires you to reject the AI’s recommendation and include the communities it overlooked.

12.2.3 Shura — Consultation

Shura means consultation. It is a Qur’anic principle: “And those who have responded to their Lord and established prayer and whose affair is [determined by] consultation among themselves” (Ash-Shura 42:38).[^12] In the Bangsamoro context, the BTA Parliament itself is an expression of shura — a body where representatives deliberate collectively before making decisions.

Applied to AI, shura requires that you consult before you deploy. AI adoption is not a unilateral decision. If you plan to use AI to change how your office prepares budget proposals, the budget staff should have a voice. If you plan to use AI to screen job applications, the HR team and the applicants’ representatives should be consulted. If you plan to use AI to analyze community development data, the communities whose data you are analyzing should know.

Shura applies at three levels:

  1. Within your team. Before introducing AI into any workflow, discuss it with the people who do the work. Ask what they think. Ask what concerns them. Ask what tasks they wish AI could help with. The people closest to the work understand the risks best.
  2. Within your institution. Before scaling AI use beyond a single team, present the results to leadership. Share what worked. Share what failed. Propose a policy. Shura is not asking permission — it is building informed consensus.
  3. With the public. When AI affects how citizens experience government services — when it shapes which communities receive resources, how complaints are processed, or how data about people is analyzed — the public has a right to know. This is not a theoretical principle. BOL Article IX establishes the right to adequate social services.[^13] If AI changes how those services are delivered, transparency is mandatory.

The shura test for AI adoption: Before deploying AI in any government process, can you answer “yes” to all three questions?

  • Have the people affected by this AI use been consulted?
  • Have their concerns been heard and addressed?
  • Would this AI use survive public scrutiny?

If the answer to any question is “no,” go back to consultation.

12.2.4 Maslahah — Public Interest

Maslahah means public interest or public benefit. In Islamic jurisprudence, maslahah is the principle that governance decisions must serve the common good. It is the ultimate test for any government action: does this benefit the people?

Applied to AI, maslahah asks a direct question: Does this AI application make government work better for the people of Bangsamoro? Not better for the ministry’s internal process. Not better for the statistics in an annual report. Not better for the staff’s convenience. Better for the people.

The maslahah test has three components:

  1. Benefit. Does this AI use produce a tangible benefit for the public? Faster service delivery? More accurate budget analysis? Better-informed legislation? If the benefit is purely internal efficiency with no public-facing improvement, reconsider.
  2. Harm avoidance. Does this AI use create risks for any community? Could it lead to biased resource allocation? Could it expose personal data? Could it reduce human contact in services where human judgment is essential? Maslahah requires that you prevent harm before pursuing benefit.
  3. Proportionality. Is the benefit proportional to the cost and risk? If deploying an AI tool requires a significant technology investment but serves only one office’s convenience, maslahah may not be satisfied. If the same investment improves service delivery across five provinces, the calculus changes.

BARMM application: A ministry proposes using AI to automate the processing of social welfare applications. The maslahah analysis looks like this:

  • Benefit: Processing time drops from 30 days to 7 days. More families receive assistance faster. Maslahah is served.
  • Harm risk: The AI screening algorithm may reject applications from families who lack documentation — precisely the most vulnerable families. Maslahah is violated.
  • Resolution: Use AI to accelerate processing but keep human reviewers for rejected applications. Require that every denial receives a manual second review within 48 hours. Maslahah is restored.

Table 12.2: Islamic Principles as AI Guardrails

Principle Arabic Core Requirement AI Guardrail Violation Example
Amanah Trust Protect what is entrusted to you Protect data; verify outputs; disclose AI use Uploading confidential documents to an unsecured AI tool
Adl Justice Treat all people fairly Audit AI outputs for bias against any community AI-generated budget analysis that consistently underfunds island provinces
Shura Consultation Consult before deciding Involve affected parties before deploying AI Implementing AI-based service screening without consulting the communities being screened
Maslahah Public Interest Serve the common good Evaluate every AI use against public benefit Using AI to optimize internal workflows while citizens still wait months for services

12.3 The Enhanced 12-Point Priority Agenda and AI Alignment

The BARMM government operates under an Enhanced 12-Point Priority Agenda that defines the transition period’s strategic direction.[^14] Each priority area creates opportunities for AI — and each one also creates risks if AI is used without ethical guardrails.

Table 12.3 maps all twelve priorities to AI alignment opportunities and the ethical boundaries that apply.

Table 12.3: 12-Point Priority Agenda with AI Alignment

# Priority Agenda AI Alignment Opportunity Ethical Boundary
1 Bureaucracy — Strengthen the regional bureaucracy AI-assisted organizational analysis, staffing pattern optimization, workload distribution Do not use AI to replace positions that serve vulnerable communities. Efficiency gains must translate to better service, not smaller workforce.
2 Priority Codes — Enact seven priority codes AI-assisted legislative drafting, cross-referencing, and legal harmonization (Chapter 7) All AI-drafted provisions must be verified against the BOL and existing BAAs by human legal staff. Never submit AI-drafted legislation without expert review.
3 Revenue Generation — Increase revenue and economic advantages AI-assisted revenue analysis, tax collection pattern identification, fiscal forecasting Tax analysis must not disproportionately target small businesses in one province while overlooking compliance gaps elsewhere. Adl applies.
4 Social Services — Improve education, health, social welfare AI-assisted service delivery analysis, resource allocation modeling, beneficiary targeting AI must not become a gatekeeper that denies services. Keep human decision-makers in all denial decisions. Maslahah requires this.
5 Infrastructure — Develop strategic infrastructure AI-assisted project prioritization, procurement documentation, progress monitoring Infrastructure prioritization must include island and remote provinces, not just areas with the most available data.
6 Economic Development — Foster inclusive economic growth AI-assisted MSME analysis, cooperative development planning, economic zone modeling “Inclusive” means all 13 ethnolinguistic groups.[^15] Check whether AI-generated economic strategies account for Teduray, Lambangian, Dulangan Manobo, and B’laan communities alongside Maguindanaon, Meranao, and Tausug populations.
7 Environment — Protect natural resources AI-assisted environmental monitoring, satellite data analysis, compliance tracking Environmental analysis must incorporate indigenous knowledge. Non-Moro IP communities have stewardship traditions that predate modern environmental science.
8 Disaster Resilience — Build community resilience AI-assisted risk mapping, early warning analysis, post-disaster resource coordination Disaster response algorithms must prioritize vulnerability, not connectivity. Communities without internet access are often the most at risk.
9 Peace and Normalization — Implement peace agreement provisions AI-assisted tracking of normalization commitments, decommissioning progress analysis Peace process data is inherently sensitive. Do not upload decommissioning records, combatant lists, or transitional justice documents to any external AI tool.
10 Marawi Rehabilitation — Support Marawi City recovery AI-assisted rehabilitation planning, land title analysis, displacement tracking Marawi data involves displaced families, contested land, and deep trauma. Handle with maximum sensitivity. Human review for every output.
11 Peace and Security — Sustain peace, justice, and security AI-assisted security pattern analysis, conflict early warning Security data must never be processed through commercial AI tools. Classify it and keep it within government-controlled systems.
12 Cultural Heritage — Preserve Bangsamoro culture and identity AI-assisted cultural documentation, tarsila (genealogy) digitization, heritage mapping Cultural heritage belongs to the communities. Do not digitize cultural materials without community consent. Do not train AI models on cultural content without permission.

The table reveals a consistent pattern. Every priority agenda item benefits from AI assistance — and every one requires human judgment to prevent AI from violating the very values the agenda is meant to serve. There is no priority area where AI can operate without guardrails.

12.4 Risks and Safeguards

AI adoption in BARMM governance creates five categories of risk. Each one is real, each one is already present in some form, and each one requires specific safeguards.

Risk 1: Bias

What it is. AI systems reflect the biases in their training data. Large language models are trained predominantly on English-language internet text written by people in the Global North. This means AI has limited understanding of Bangsamoro governance, culture, and context. When it generates analysis about BARMM, it fills gaps with generalizations that may be wrong.

How it appears. An AI-generated policy analysis recommends governance reforms based on best practices from countries with established democracies. The recommendations ignore that BARMM is in a transition period, operates under a parliamentary system within a presidential framework, and has a unique intergovernmental relationship with the national government through the BOL.[^16] The recommendations sound sophisticated but are irrelevant.

Safeguard. Always provide BARMM-specific context in your prompts. Supply the relevant BAA text, BDP chapter, or institutional data. Do not rely on the AI’s general knowledge of Philippine governance — it is incomplete. Review every output for assumptions that do not match BARMM reality.

Risk 2: Data Sovereignty

What it is. When you upload government documents to a commercial AI tool, the data leaves your control. Most AI providers process data on servers outside the Philippines. Some retain uploaded data for model training. This creates a sovereignty risk: government data about Bangsamoro citizens, budgets, and operations may be stored on foreign servers with no guarantee of privacy.

How it appears. A ministry uploads five years of provincial budget data to an AI tool for analysis. The AI provider’s terms of service allow data retention for “service improvement.” The budget data — including granular allocations by municipality — now sits on servers outside the Philippines, accessible to an unknown number of the provider’s employees.

Safeguard. Establish a data classification system. Define which documents can be uploaded to external AI tools and which must stay within government-controlled systems. As a baseline:

  • Public documents (published BAAs, resolutions, public reports): May be uploaded.
  • Internal working documents (draft policies, internal memos, staff records): Upload only with anonymization.
  • Confidential documents (security data, personnel records with PII, financial audit details): Do not upload to external AI tools under any circumstances.

Risk 3: Over-Dependency

What it is. When AI produces good outputs consistently, staff may stop developing the skills that make those outputs possible. If the AI drafts every briefer, staff may lose the ability to draft one from scratch. If the AI summarizes every audit report, staff may lose the ability to read one critically.

How it appears. A new legislative analyst joins the team. She learns to use AI from Day 1. Two years later, the AI tool’s subscription lapses. She cannot draft a legislative briefer without it — because she never learned the underlying analytical process. The tool replaced the skill instead of augmenting it.

Safeguard. Use the “apprentice model” described in the Supervision Guidebook. AI is the journeyman assistant, not the master. Staff must learn the craft first and use AI to amplify what they know.[^17] Periodically require manual work to ensure skills remain sharp. Include “AI-off” exercises in training programs.

Risk 4: Digital Divide

What it is. AI tools require internet access, modern devices, and digital literacy. Not all BARMM staff have all three. Island provinces face connectivity challenges.[^18] Older staff may have limited experience with digital tools. Staff in remote field offices may lack the hardware to run AI applications.

How it appears. A ministry’s central office in Cotabato City adopts AI tools and sees productivity improve dramatically. The provincial offices in Tawi-Tawi and Basilan do not have reliable internet. A two-tier bureaucracy emerges: fast, AI-augmented decision-making in the center; slow, manual processing in the periphery. The periphery — which serves the most underserved communities — falls further behind.

Safeguard. Design AI adoption with the periphery in mind. If AI tools require internet connectivity, pair them with offline alternatives. If training is available only in the central office, create recorded sessions for field staff. Track AI adoption rates by geographic location and address gaps proactively. The 12-Point Priority Agenda’s commitment to inclusivity (agenda item 12) demands this.

Risk 5: Inequality

What it is. AI adoption may create power asymmetries within government. Offices that adopt AI early gain advantages in document production, analysis speed, and institutional knowledge management. Offices that do not adopt AI fall behind. Over time, this creates a competitive imbalance that concentrates capability — and influence — in a few units.

How it appears. The Office of the Chief Minister’s policy staff uses AI extensively. They produce comprehensive analyses faster than any ministry. Over time, ministries begin to defer to OCM analysis rather than developing their own. Institutional capacity concentrates at the top while ministries — the entities closest to service delivery — weaken.

Safeguard. Distribute AI training and resources equitably. Ensure that every ministry, not just central offices, has access to AI tools and the skills to use them. The Chapter 13 implementation roadmap addresses this through a phased adoption plan that prioritizes breadth over depth.

12.5 A Bangsamoro AI Ethics Framework

No existing AI ethics framework was designed for BARMM. Global frameworks from the EU, UNESCO, and OECD address AI governance in general terms but do not account for the Islamic governance tradition, the BOL’s unique federal-asymmetric structure, or the specific challenges of a region in political transition.[^19]

BARMM needs its own framework. What follows is a proposed set of principles — grounded in the moral foundations already discussed in this chapter — that can guide AI adoption across the Bangsamoro government.

The Seven Principles of Bangsamoro AI Ethics

Principle 1: Human Authority Over AI (Khalifah)

AI advises. Humans decide. No AI output replaces the judgment of an accountable public servant. The concept of khalifah (stewardship/vicegerency) in Islam means that humans are entrusted with authority on earth. AI is a tool within that trust — not a co-decision-maker.

Practical application: Every AI-assisted government process must have a designated human decision-maker whose name and designation appear on the final output.

Principle 2: Truthfulness in AI-Assisted Outputs (Sidq)

All AI-assisted documents must be verified for accuracy before submission. Sidq (truthfulness) is a non-negotiable Islamic value. Presenting AI-generated content as verified fact without actually verifying it violates sidq.

Practical application: Implement the verification protocol from Chapter 3. Check every citation, every data point, every attributed quote. If the AI output contains information you cannot verify, remove it or flag it as unverified.

Principle 3: Justice in AI Application (Adl)

AI must serve all communities equitably. No AI application should systematically advantage one ethnic group, province, gender, or socioeconomic class over another. If bias is detected, the AI application must be corrected or withdrawn.

Practical application: After any AI-assisted analysis that involves resource allocation, service delivery, or policy targeting, conduct a bias check. Ask: Which communities are included in this analysis? Which are missing? Would a Teduray community in the Maguindanao highlands experience this policy recommendation the same way a Meranao community in Marawi City would?

Principle 4: Protection of Trust (Amanah)

Government data is a public trust. It must be protected with the same diligence that applies to public funds. Data classification, access controls, and privacy protections apply to all AI-processed government information.

Practical application: Classify all government documents before processing them with AI tools. Maintain an AI usage log that records what documents were processed, which AI tool was used, and who authorized the processing.

Principle 5: Consultation Before Deployment (Shura)

No AI system should be deployed in a government process without consulting the people affected. This applies to government staff whose workflows will change, to communities whose data will be analyzed, and to Parliament when AI affects how laws are implemented.

Practical application: Before any ministry adopts AI for a new process, convene a consultation meeting with the relevant division staff. Document the consultation. Record concerns and how they were addressed.

Principle 6: Public Benefit as the Standard (Maslahah)

Every AI application in BARMM governance must demonstrate a public benefit. Internal efficiency that does not translate to better public service does not satisfy this principle. The benefit must be measurable and must reach the communities the government serves.

Practical application: For each AI adoption proposal, require a one-page maslahah assessment: What is the public benefit? Who benefits? Who might be harmed? How will harm be prevented? This assessment accompanies the adoption decision.

Principle 7: Transparency and Accountability (Hisab)

Hisab means accountability — the awareness that all actions will be accounted for. In AI governance, this means full transparency about how AI is used, what decisions it informs, and who is responsible for the outcomes.

Practical application: Publish an annual AI transparency report for each ministry or office that uses AI tools. The report lists: which AI tools are used, for what purposes, how many documents were AI-assisted, what safeguards are in place, and what issues were identified and corrected.

Summary Table: The Seven Principles

# Principle Arabic Root One-Sentence Rule
1 Human Authority Khalifah AI advises; humans decide and sign.
2 Truthfulness Sidq Verify every AI output before submission.
3 Justice Adl Check every AI output for bias against any community.
4 Protection of Trust Amanah Classify data and protect it before AI processes it.
5 Consultation Shura Consult affected people before deploying AI.
6 Public Benefit Maslahah Every AI use must serve the public, not just the office.
7 Accountability Hisab Log, disclose, and report all government AI use.

12.6 When AI Must Not Decide

Throughout this guidebook, the principle has been consistent: AI prepares; humans decide. But some decisions carry such weight — moral, legal, cultural, or political — that AI should not even prepare the ground without extraordinary caution. In some cases, AI must be excluded entirely.

This section draws a clear boundary.

Category 1: Shari’ah Rulings

AI must never issue, simulate, or recommend Shari’ah rulings. The Bangsamoro justice system includes Shari’ah courts with jurisdiction over personal law — marriage, divorce, inheritance, custody — and property relations among Muslims.[^20] These courts draw their authority from the Qur’an and Sunnah. Their rulings require qualified Shari’ah judges trained in Islamic jurisprudence.

AI does not have Islamic jurisprudential training. It cannot weigh competing interpretations of hadith. It cannot consider the specific circumstances of a family dispute with the pastoral sensitivity that Islamic law requires. It cannot issue a ruling that carries religious and legal authority.

What AI may do: Organize case records for Shari’ah court clerks. Compile relevant precedents from prior court decisions. Format pleadings and legal documents. These are clerical functions.

What AI must not do: Recommend a ruling. Suggest how Islamic law should apply to a specific case. Generate a fatwa or anything resembling one. Draft a Shari’ah court decision.

Category 2: Political Negotiations

AI must not participate in political negotiations. The Bangsamoro peace process — from the Comprehensive Agreement on the Bangsamoro (2014) through the BOL’s ratification (2019) and the ongoing normalization process — was built on human trust between parties who fought each other for decades.[^21] Political negotiations within the BTA — coalition-building, committee assignments, legislative bargaining — depend on relationships, cultural norms, and political judgment that no AI possesses.

What AI may do: Prepare background research for negotiators. Summarize historical agreements. Compare provisions across different drafts. These are research functions.

What AI must not do: Recommend negotiation positions. Predict political outcomes. Draft compromise language without explicit human direction on the terms of the compromise.

Category 3: Cultural Determinations

AI must not make cultural judgments. The Bangsamoro region includes 13 ethnolinguistic groups with distinct traditions, customary laws, and cultural heritage.[^22] Determining whether a cultural practice should be preserved, how a tradition should be represented, or whether a heritage site qualifies for protection requires cultural expertise that AI does not have.

What AI may do: Assist in cultural documentation — transcribing oral histories, organizing artifact catalogs, mapping heritage sites.

What AI must not do: Determine the cultural significance of a practice. Recommend which traditions merit preservation. Represent any ethnolinguistic group’s cultural values or positions.

Category 4: Disciplinary Actions

AI must not recommend disciplinary actions against government personnel. The Bangsamoro Civil Service Code establishes due process requirements for administrative proceedings.[^23] Disciplinary actions affect livelihoods, reputations, and careers. They require the procedural safeguards that only human judgment can provide.

What AI may do: Compile attendance records, performance data, and prior corrective actions to support a supervisor’s review.

What AI must not do: Recommend termination, suspension, or demotion. Assess whether an employee’s behavior constitutes a violation. Draft the finding of fact in an administrative case.

Category 5: Decisions Affecting Indigenous Peoples’ Rights

AI must not make decisions affecting the rights of non-Moro indigenous peoples. BOL Article IX recognizes indigenous peoples’ rights.[^24] The Teduray, Lambangian, Dulangan Manobo, and B’laan communities within BARMM have distinct governance traditions and customary laws that predate both Philippine national law and Islamic governance structures.[^25]

What AI may do: Assist in documenting IP customary laws and governance practices. Compile data for IP community development plans.

What AI must not do: Determine land use boundaries in ancestral domains. Recommend policies that affect IP communities without those communities’ direct participation. Generate analysis that treats non-Moro IPs as a footnote to Bangsamoro governance.

Category 6: Life-and-Death Decisions

AI must not participate in decisions that directly affect human life and safety. Disaster response triage, security threat assessments, and public health emergency decisions must remain fully human.

What AI may do: Compile weather data, analyze historical disaster patterns, and organize evacuation logistics information.

What AI must not do: Determine which communities receive emergency aid first. Assess which security threats are credible. Decide which patients receive scarce medical resources.

The Boundaries List

For quick reference, post this list in your office alongside Table 12.1:

AI MUST NOT:

  1. Issue, simulate, or recommend Shari’ah rulings
  2. Recommend political negotiation positions
  3. Make cultural judgments about Bangsamoro heritage
  4. Recommend disciplinary actions against personnel
  5. Determine policies affecting indigenous peoples’ rights without IP participation
  6. Make decisions that directly affect human life and safety
  7. Replace the signature authority of any accountable public servant
  8. Operate without a designated human decision-maker
  9. Process classified security or peace-process documents on external systems
  10. Deny government services to citizens without human review

If you are unsure whether AI should be involved in a decision, apply this test: Would you be comfortable if a newspaper reported that this decision was made with AI assistance? If the answer is no — or even “maybe” — keep AI out of it.

Chapter Summary

This chapter established the ethical foundation for AI adoption in BARMM governance. The foundation is not imported. It is Bangsamoro.

The 12 Pillars of Moral Governance apply to AI just as they apply to every other government tool. Table 12.1 maps each pillar to a specific AI governance obligation. Transparency and Honesty — Pillar 3 — requires that every AI-assisted document be labeled, that AI’s contribution be identified, and that human verification be confirmed.

Four Islamic principles — amanah, adl, shura, and maslahah — create guardrails that predate AI by over a millennium. Amanah protects data. Adl prevents bias. Shura requires consultation. Maslahah demands public benefit.

The Enhanced 12-Point Priority Agenda benefits from AI in every priority area — and requires ethical boundaries in every priority area. Table 12.3 maps the opportunities and the limits.

Five categories of risk — bias, data sovereignty, over-dependency, digital divide, and inequality — require specific safeguards. None of these risks is hypothetical. All are present today.

Seven principles constitute a proposed Bangsamoro AI Ethics Framework, rooted in Islamic governance and Moral Governance traditions. Each principle is actionable. Each one names a specific obligation.

Six categories of decisions must exclude AI entirely or restrict it to clerical support: Shari’ah rulings, political negotiations, cultural determinations, disciplinary actions, IP rights decisions, and life-and-death decisions.

The principle that runs through this entire guidebook reaches its sharpest expression in this chapter: AI is a tool. You are the authority. The ethics are yours.

Footnotes

[^1]: The 12 Pillars of Moral Governance are drawn from the 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 5: Improving and Strengthening Governance.

[^2]: 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 5, Section on “Twelve Pillars of Moral Governance.”

[^3]: Republic Act No. 11054 (Bangsamoro Organic Law), Article IV, General Principles and Policies. Signed July 27, 2018; ratified January-February 2019.

[^4]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article X, Section 1. The Shari’ah justice system recognizes the Qur’an and Sunnah as principal sources.

[^5]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article IX, Section 24.

[^6]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article VIII. The Wali is the ceremonial head representing the moral authority of the Bangsamoro people.

[^7]: Supervision in Bangsamoro Governance: A Competency-Based Guidebook for BARMM Supervisors, Chapter 1: “The Supervisor’s Role in Bangsamoro Governance,” Section 1.1.1 (Islamic Leadership Principles — Amanah). The supervision guidebook defines amanah as the ethical foundation of all supervisory responsibility in BARMM.

[^8]: Republic Act No. 10173 (Data Privacy Act of 2012). This national law applies within BARMM and governs the processing of personal information by government agencies.

[^9]: Hadith narrated by Abdullah ibn Umar, collected in Sahih al-Bukhari (No. 7138) and Sahih Muslim (No. 1829).

[^10]: The Qur’an, Surah An-Nisa (4:135). Translation follows the Sahih International rendering.

[^11]: The 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity), identifies 13 ethnolinguistic groups comprising 11 Islamized and 4 non-Moro indigenous peoples within the Bangsamoro.

[^12]: The Qur’an, Surah Ash-Shura (42:38). Translation follows the Sahih International rendering.

[^13]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article IX (Basic Rights). Section 24 specifically addresses the right to culture and identity.

[^14]: 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 5, Section on “Enhanced 12-Point Priority Agenda.”

[^15]: 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity). The 13 ethnolinguistic groups include 11 Islamized groups (Maguindanaon, Meranao, Iranun, Tausug, Sama, Yakan, Sangil, Molbog, Jama Mapun, Kalagan, Kalibugan) and 4 non-Moro indigenous peoples (Teduray, Lambangian, Dulangan Manobo, B’laan).

[^16]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article VI (Intergovernmental Relations) establishes mechanisms for coordination between the national government and the Bangsamoro government, including the Intergovernmental Relations Body (IGRB).

[^17]: Supervision in Bangsamoro Governance: A Competency-Based Guidebook for BARMM Supervisors, Chapter 1. The guidebook frames the supervisory role as one of developing staff capacity, not just managing outputs.

[^18]: 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 8 (Harnessing Technology and Innovations). The BDP notes infrastructure challenges particularly in island provinces.

[^19]: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021); OECD Principles on AI (2019); EU AI Act (2024). These frameworks address AI governance in general terms but do not account for Islamic governance traditions or the specific constitutional structure of autonomous regions within unitary states.

[^20]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article X, Sections 1-10. Shari’ah courts have jurisdiction over personal law (marriage, divorce, inheritance, custody) and property relations among Muslims within BARMM.

[^21]: The Comprehensive Agreement on the Bangsamoro (CAB), signed March 27, 2014, between the Government of the Philippines and the Moro Islamic Liberation Front (MILF), provided the framework for the BOL.

[^22]: 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity).

[^23]: Bangsamoro Autonomy Act No. 17 (Bangsamoro Civil Service Code), enacted February 2021. Establishes personnel management standards, including due process for administrative proceedings.

[^24]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article IX (Basic Rights). Section 24 recognizes the rights of indigenous peoples within BARMM.

[^25]: 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 6. The non-Moro indigenous peoples — Teduray, Lambangian, Dulangan Manobo, and B’laan — maintain distinct governance traditions and customary laws including the Walay na Bityara (Maguindanaon) and Taritib ago Igma (Meranao) systems.

Back to Table of Contents
13
Getting Started: An Implementation Roadmap

Chapter 13 — Getting Started: An Implementation Roadmap

You have read eleven chapters on what AI can do across the governance cycle. You have seen prompts, examples, and BARMM-specific applications. The question now is: where do you start?

The answer depends on where you are. A staff member opening Claude for the first time faces a different challenge than a ministry director drafting an AI adoption policy. A lone researcher experimenting with NotebookLM operates in a different reality than a Parliament committee secretariat standardizing AI-assisted briefers across twelve standing committees.

This chapter gives you the roadmap. It maps three maturity levels — individual, team, institutional — and provides concrete actions for each. It ends with a 90-day plan that any BARMM office can adapt, and a change management framework that addresses the specific resistance patterns you will encounter in Bangsamoro government settings.

Start where you are. Move at the speed your context allows. But start.

13.1 The Three Maturity Levels

AI adoption does not happen all at once. It follows a predictable progression. Understanding where you fall on this progression prevents two common mistakes: trying to do too much too fast (which creates backlash and abandoned tools) and staying at the individual level forever (which limits AI’s impact to personal productivity rather than institutional improvement).

13.1.1 Level 1: Individual Use and Personal Productivity

Level 1 is one person using AI to do their own work faster. No policy exists. No team agreement governs usage. The staff member experiments on their own initiative, learns through trial and error, and applies AI to tasks within their personal control.

Characteristics of Level 1:

  • One user, one tool. A legislative researcher uses Claude to summarize committee reports. A budget analyst uses NotebookLM to analyze COA findings. Each person works independently.
  • No shared prompts or standards. Two staff members doing the same task may prompt differently and get inconsistent outputs.
  • No institutional knowledge capture. When a staff member discovers an effective prompt for drafting BAA summaries, that knowledge stays in their head or their personal files.
