Estonia: Digital Governance for a Small State. Estonia, with a population of 1.3 million (roughly comparable to BARMM’s Maguindanao del Norte), built the world’s most advanced digital governance ecosystem.[^6] Its X-Road data exchange layer connects every government database. Its e-Residency program allows digital participation in government services from anywhere. Its Kratt initiative (the Estonian word for a magical servant in folklore) applies AI to automate routine administrative decisions — tax assessments, parking fines, agricultural subsidies — with full transparency about when AI is involved.[^7]

The lesson for BARMM: small governments have an advantage in digital transformation. Estonia’s success came precisely because it was small enough to build interconnected systems. BARMM has 16 ministries, not 200 federal agencies. It has one parliament, not two legislative chambers. Its bureaucracy is young enough that legacy systems have not calcified. The same smallness that makes BARMM’s capacity thin also makes its systems reformable.

Table 15.1: Global AI Governance Models and BARMM Application

Dimension Singapore UAE Estonia BARMM Opportunity
Population 5.9 million 9.4 million 1.3 million 4.4 million
AI strategy National AI Strategy 2.0 (2023) UAE AI Strategy (2017, updated 2023) Kratt AI Initiative (2019) No formal AI strategy yet
Institutional owner Smart Nation and Digital Government Office Minister of State for AI Government CIO Office MOST and BPDA (potential owners)
Key platform OneService, Ask Jamie DubaiNow (130+ services) X-Road data exchange e-Bangsamoro (in development)
Entry point Cross-agency service routing Consolidated service delivery Automated administrative decisions AI-augmented document workflows
Budget investment S$1 billion over 5 years AED 300 billion target by 2031 Less than EUR 100 million (phased) Minimal — tools already available
BARMM lesson Solve specific bottlenecks first Create institutional ownership Small scale enables faster reform All three lessons apply

What These Benchmarks Mean for BARMM

The gap between BARMM and these three countries is not technology. Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, and NotebookLM are available to any BARMM staff member with an internet connection. The gap is institutional intent — a formal decision, backed by policy, that AI-augmented workflows are how this government will operate.

The BDP 2023-2028 dedicates Chapter 8 to science, technology, and innovation.[^8] As Chapter 1 of this guidebook documented, that chapter focuses almost entirely on physical infrastructure — science labs, research scholarships, a science park. It identifies the need for research and development capacity and technology-based solutions but does not address AI as a governance tool or digital transformation as a public administration strategy.[^9] The next development plan — whether it covers 2028-2034 or a different horizon — must include digital governance and AI-augmented public administration as explicit strategy areas. Not as aspirational language. As programs, activities, and projects with budgets, timelines, and performance indicators.

Three concrete steps bridge the BDP gap:

  1. Include “Digital Governance” as a strategy area under BDP Goal 6 (Responsive, Transparent, and Accountable Governance). This creates a policy home for AI adoption, digital platforms, and data-driven decision-making within the development planning framework.
  2. Assign MOST as lead agency with BPDA as coordinating body. MOST provides the technical capacity; BPDA ensures alignment with broader development goals and monitors implementation across ministries.
  3. Set measurable indicators. Percentage of ministries with formal AI use policies. Number of staff who have completed AI literacy training. Reduction in average document processing time for standard governance outputs.

15.2 Digital Governance and e-Bangsamoro — How AI Connects to Digital Transformation

The Platform Already Under Construction

Throughout this guidebook, you have used AI as a standalone productivity tool — prompting Claude or ChatGPT from your laptop, uploading documents manually, reviewing outputs on your screen. That is Level 1 (Individual Use) and Level 2 (Team Integration) from Chapter 13’s maturity model. Level 3 — Institutional Adoption — requires something more: a digital platform where AI is embedded into government workflows, not bolted on as a separate tool.

That platform is e-Bangsamoro.

e-Bangsamoro is a four-portal e-governance system currently under development for the Bangsamoro government.[^10] Its architecture reflects the governance structure of BARMM itself:

  1. Parliamentary Portal. Legislation tracking, bill drafting workflows, committee management, resolution filing, plenary session records. Every AI-assisted legislative workflow from Chapter 7 and Chapter 11 maps to this portal.

  2. Ministerial Portal. Program management, implementation tracking, reporting dashboards, procurement documentation. Every AI-assisted implementation workflow from Chapter 8 maps here.

  3. Budget Portal. Budget preparation, authorization tracking, execution monitoring, COA audit integration. Every AI-assisted budgeting workflow from Chapter 6 and every oversight workflow from Chapter 10 maps here.

  4. Citizen Portal. Service requests, transparency dashboards, public document access, feedback mechanisms. The open government and transparency tools from Chapter 10 connect to this portal.

How AI Transforms a Digital Platform

A digital platform without AI is a database with a user interface. Staff enter data. The system stores it. Reports come out. This is useful but incremental — it digitizes paper processes without fundamentally changing how work gets done.

A digital platform with AI changes the workflow itself:

Without AI With AI
Staff manually search 89 BAAs for relevant provisions AI scans the full corpus and returns cross-referenced results in seconds
Budget analysts compare spreadsheets row by row AI identifies anomalies, generates trend analysis, and flags underspending patterns automatically
Legislative staff draft briefers from scratch AI generates structured first drafts from uploaded committee hearing transcripts and reference documents
Citizen complaints sit in a queue until a clerk routes them AI classifies complaints by category, urgency, and responsible ministry, then routes them automatically
M&E officers compile quarterly reports from 16 ministry submissions AI aggregates data, identifies performance gaps, and generates dashboard visualizations
HR staff screen hundreds of applications manually AI compares applicant qualifications against position standards and generates shortlist recommendations for human review

The Digital Governance Ecosystem

e-Bangsamoro does not operate in isolation. It connects to a broader ecosystem of digital governance tools that the Bangsamoro government will build over the coming decade:

  • BangsamoroHR. Human resource management across the BARMM bureaucracy — staffing patterns, competency tracking, training records, performance evaluation.
  • Tarbiyyah-MS. Madrasah management — school records, asatidz (teacher) data, student enrollment, curriculum tracking for the BARMM’s Islamic education system.
  • MoroMarket (e-Negosyo). A digital marketplace connecting Bangsamoro cooperatives, social enterprises, and MSMEs to buyers.
  • SBP (School Building Program). Infrastructure tracking for school construction and rehabilitation across BARMM.
  • IPP (Integrated Planning Platform). Strategic planning, program design, and BDP alignment for ministries and offices.

Each of these platforms generates data. AI connects the data across platforms. A budget portal that knows how many teachers Tarbiyyah-MS tracks can cross-reference staffing costs. A parliamentary portal that knows how many school buildings SBP reports can verify implementation claims during committee hearings. An HR system that knows which staff completed AI literacy training can feed into the capacity-building indicators that Chapter 14 defined. AI is the connective tissue of digital governance.

From Guidebook Workflows to Platform Features

Every AI workflow you learned in this guidebook has a natural home in the e-Bangsamoro ecosystem:

Guidebook Chapter AI Workflow Platform Destination
Ch 5 — Strategic Planning AI-assisted BDP alignment, Theory of Change design IPP
Ch 6 — Budgeting Budget analysis, fiscal forecasting, COA compliance Budget Portal
Ch 7 — Legislative Work Bill drafting, legislative briefers, cross-referencing BAAs Parliamentary Portal
Ch 8 — Implementation Project management, procurement documentation Ministerial Portal
Ch 9 — M&E Performance monitoring, data visualization Ministerial Portal + Budget Portal
Ch 10 — Oversight Audit analysis, transparency reporting Parliamentary Portal + Citizen Portal
Ch 11 — Codification Legal harmonization, IRR development Parliamentary Portal
Ch 12 — Ethics AI ethics framework, Shari’ah compliance review Cross-platform governance layer
Ch 14 — Training AI literacy tracking, competency assessment BangsamoroHR

The path from where you are now (using AI as a personal productivity tool) to where BARMM needs to be (AI-embedded institutional workflows) runs through the digital platforms. This guidebook taught you the skills. The platforms will scale them.

The Infrastructure Question

Digital platforms require infrastructure that BARMM is still building. Internet connectivity in island provinces remains inconsistent. Government offices in some municipalities still rely on paper-first processes. Power supply interruptions affect server availability.

These constraints are real. They do not invalidate the vision — they sequence it. The implementation follows the same maturity model from Chapter 13:

  • Phase 1 (Level 1-2). AI tools run on individual devices using commercial cloud services. Staff in Cotabato City, where connectivity is strongest, pilot workflows first. No custom infrastructure required.
  • Phase 2 (Level 2-3). Shared team platforms (NotebookLM, Obsidian vaults, shared prompt libraries) operate alongside e-Bangsamoro development. AI features are prototyped and tested.
  • Phase 3 (Level 3). e-Bangsamoro and companion platforms deploy with AI embedded. Infrastructure investments — government data centers, improved connectivity, backup power — support sustained operations.

The BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 8 acknowledges that BARMM’s ICT infrastructure needs strengthening.[^11] AI adoption does not wait for perfect infrastructure. It starts with what exists and grows with what is built.

15.3 AI and the First Regular Government — Hitting the Ground Running

The 2026 Transition

The Bangsamoro Transition Authority was established by the BOL as a transitional government to operate until the first regular elections.[^12] Those elections are scheduled for 2025, with the first regular Bangsamoro government taking office in 2025-2026.[^13] This transition is historic. For the first time, the Bangsamoro people will elect their own parliament and chief minister under the BOL framework.

The incoming government inherits a substantial legislative and institutional foundation:

  • 89 Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts enacted by the BTA Parliament.[^14]
  • Over 570 resolutions addressing governance, policy, and parliamentary operations.
  • 16 ministries and multiple executive offices with established (if sometimes understaffed) organizational structures.
  • A development framework — the BDP 2023-2028 — with six goals, eight strategies, and quantified targets.
  • A fiscal system governed by BAA 84 (Budget System Act) and the annual General Appropriations Act.

But the incoming government also inherits challenges that AI directly addresses:

Challenge 1: Institutional Knowledge Loss

When an elected government replaces a transition authority, staff turnover is inevitable. Political appointees change. Chiefs of staff rotate. Legislative researchers who drafted bills for six years move to different offices. The institutional knowledge they carry — why a particular provision was worded that way, which ministry resisted an earlier version of a bill, what COA flagged in the 2023 audit — walks out the door with them.

AI mitigates this through searchable institutional memory. Chapter 10 described how to build AI-searchable knowledge bases from committee records, audit findings, and legislative histories. An incoming MP who asks, “What happened with the fisheries bill?” can query a system that returns the full legislative history — including committee deliberations, amendments, voting records, and implementation status — instead of relying on a departing staff member’s memory.

Challenge 2: Legislative Backlog

The first regular parliament will inherit a backlog. Some BAAs need amendment based on implementation experience. IRRs for recently enacted laws need promulgation. Proposed bills that did not pass the BTA will be refiled. New legislative priorities from the elected government’s platform will compete for committee time.

AI accelerates the legislative pipeline. The bill drafting, legislative briefing, and codification workflows from Chapters 7 and 11 allow a smaller legislative staff to process a larger volume of legislation. A newly elected MP with a mandate to file five priority bills can use AI to produce structured first drafts within weeks, not months.

Challenge 3: Budget Execution Pressure

An elected government faces immediate pressure to deliver results. Constituents who voted for specific candidates expect visible improvements — in schools, roads, health services, livelihood programs. The budget cycle does not wait. The first regular government must prepare a General Appropriations Act almost immediately after taking office.

AI makes budget analysis faster and more rigorous. Chapter 6’s workflows enable budget staff to analyze prior-year execution, identify underspending patterns, and draft budget proposals that learn from the BTA period’s fiscal data. An incoming Chief Minister who asks, “Where did the money actually go for the past six years?” can get a data-driven answer within days.

Challenge 4: Oversight From Day One

The first regular parliament will exercise oversight over both the incoming executive and the transition government’s legacy. It will need to audit what was accomplished, what was left undone, and where funds were spent versus where they were allocated. This is an enormous analytical task.

AI handles the volume. The oversight workflows from Chapter 10 — COA report parsing, procurement pattern analysis, compliance tracking — give parliamentary committees the analytical capacity to conduct meaningful oversight immediately, without waiting months for staff to manually review years of records.

Challenge 5: Citizen Expectations for Digital Government

Voters in 2025-2026 will be younger, more connected, and more digitally literate than the population that ratified the BOL in 2019. They will expect government services to be accessible online. They will expect transparency in budget execution. They will expect responsiveness. A government that cannot meet digital expectations will face credibility gaps that erode public trust.

AI-augmented governance is not a luxury. For the first regular government, it is a baseline competency.

Challenge 6: Harmonizing BTA-era Laws with Elected Government Priorities

The 89 BAAs enacted by the BTA represent the transition authority’s legislative judgment. An elected parliament may agree with some, disagree with others, and want to amend several. The codification and harmonization workflows from Chapter 11 become operational necessities — the new parliament needs to understand the full body of existing law before it legislates further.

AI provides the analytical baseline. Cross-referencing all 89 BAAs against each other and against the BOL is a task that would take a team of legal researchers months to complete manually. AI completes the initial mapping in hours, flagging overlaps, gaps, and potential conflicts for human legal judgment.

Table 15.2: How AI Helps the First Regular Government

First 100 Days Priority AI Application Guidebook Reference
Assess BTA legacy and institutional status AI-assisted document analysis of 89 BAAs, resolutions, and ministry reports Ch 7, Ch 10
Prepare first regular GAA AI-assisted budget analysis of BTA-era fiscal data Ch 6
File priority legislation AI-assisted bill drafting and legislative briefer preparation Ch 7, Ch 11
Establish oversight committees AI-assisted COA audit analysis and compliance tracking Ch 10
Launch citizen services platform AI-augmented citizen complaint routing and transparency dashboards Ch 10, Sec 15.2
Preserve institutional knowledge AI-searchable knowledge base from BTA records Ch 10, Ch 4
Harmonize existing legislation AI-assisted cross-referencing and codification of 89 BAAs Ch 11
Establish AI governance policy Formal AI use policy with ethics framework, training mandate, and institutional ownership Ch 12, Ch 13

15.4 From Guidebook to Practice — A Challenge to Every Reader

The Honest Assessment

You have read a guidebook about using AI to improve Bangsamoro governance. You have seen sample prompts, workflow diagrams, case studies, and implementation roadmaps. You know the theory. You understand the tools. You have reference material in the appendices that you can open tomorrow morning and start using.

None of that matters if you close this guidebook and go back to working the way you worked before you opened it.

The Author’s Perspective

I wrote this guidebook from a specific position. I have drafted bills for the Bangsamoro Parliament, built AI-powered legislative research tools, managed scholarship programs, led strategic planning sessions, and supervised staff across multiple BARMM offices.[^15] I am also a software developer who has built eight government-scale platforms for Bangsamoro institutions — from e-Bangsamoro’s four-portal e-governance system to BangsamoroHR, from the Tarbiyyah madrasah management system to MoroMarket’s cooperative marketplace. I maintain 129 AI skills that power the kinds of workflows described in every chapter of this guidebook.

I share this not as credentials but as context. The workflows in this guidebook are not theoretical. They come from years of doing the actual work — drafting legislation, preparing briefers, analyzing budgets, building systems — inside the Bangsamoro government. I wrote the prompts because I use the prompts. I designed the workflows because I follow the workflows. The case studies are drawn from real legislative and administrative processes, not hypothetical scenarios.

When I started using AI tools in government work, no guidebook like this existed. I learned by experimenting — testing prompts against actual BAAs, feeding real committee hearing transcripts into AI tools, comparing AI-generated budget analysis against manual spreadsheet work. Some experiments failed. The AI hallucinated BAA numbers. It attributed provisions to wrong articles. It generated plausible but incorrect legal reasoning. Every failure taught me something about how to structure prompts, supply context, and verify outputs. The “Trust but Verify” protocol in Chapter 3 was not invented in a classroom. It was forged from real errors in real government documents.

This guidebook is the distillation of those lessons. It is the resource I wish I had when I started.

A Challenge to Staff

If you are a government employee reading this — a legislative researcher, a budget analyst, a planning officer, a program coordinator, an executive assistant — here is what I ask of you:

Pick one task you do every week. Just one. A recurring report. A regular briefer. A monthly data compilation. A standard memorandum. Choose the task you find most tedious, most time-consuming, or most repetitive.

Now go to Chapter 3. Follow the Four-Step AI Workflow. Prepare your context. Write a clear prompt. Review the output critically. Refine it. Compare the result against what you would have produced manually.

If the AI-assisted version is faster, use it next week. If it is better, make it your default. If it fails, analyze why — check whether you supplied enough context, whether your prompt was specific enough, whether you verified the output against authoritative sources.

One task. One week. That is all I ask.

The Bangsamoro government does not transform because a guidebook says it should. It transforms because individual staff members change how they work. One prompt at a time. One workflow at a time. One verified output at a time.

A Challenge to Supervisors

If you are a division chief, a director, or a minister: your staff will not adopt AI unless you demonstrate that it is acceptable. Organizational culture flows from the top. If you treat AI outputs with suspicion — if you reject AI-assisted drafts purely because they were AI-assisted, regardless of quality — your staff will stop trying.

Chapter 14 gives you the training framework. Chapter 13 gives you the implementation roadmap. Appendix D gives you a sample AI policy you can adapt for your office. What you need to supply is permission. Tell your staff that AI-assisted work is welcome. Set quality standards — the “Trust but Verify” protocol from Chapter 3 — but do not penalize experimentation.

The best supervisors I have worked with in BARMM share a common trait: they care about the quality of the output, not the method used to produce it. If an AI-assisted briefer is more thorough, better structured, and more accurately cited than a manually written one, the method should not matter. The result should.

A Challenge to Members of Parliament

If you are an incoming Member of Parliament — whether you served in the BTA or are entering office for the first time — your legislative capacity depends on your staff’s AI literacy. An MP with AI-literate staff can file better-researched bills, deliver more thorough interpellation, exercise more rigorous oversight, and respond to constituents faster.

You do not need to use AI yourself (though Chapter 2 makes it accessible if you want to). You need to ensure that your legislative office uses it. Allocate budget for AI tool subscriptions. Send your researchers to AI training. Adopt the legislative workflows from Chapter 7. Make the 13-Section Legislative Briefer from Appendix F your office standard.

The BTA Parliament enacted 89 BAAs in six years. The first regular parliament will face higher expectations with less institutional tolerance for a learning curve. AI is how your office meets those expectations.

A Challenge to the Bangsamoro Government

If you are reading this from the Office of the Chief Minister, from BPDA, from MOST, or from any office with institutional authority over technology policy: BARMM needs a formal AI strategy.

Not a vague endorsement of innovation. A strategy document with:

  • An institutional owner. Which office is responsible for AI governance across BARMM?
  • A training mandate. Which staff categories must complete AI literacy training, and by when?
  • An ethics framework. How does BARMM ensure AI use aligns with moral governance and Islamic values (Chapter 12)?
  • A procurement policy. Which AI tools are approved for government use? How are they funded?
  • Performance indicators. How does BARMM measure whether AI adoption is improving governance outcomes?
  • A digital platform roadmap. How do e-Bangsamoro and related systems evolve to embed AI into institutional workflows (Section 15.2)?
  • A capacity building plan. How does the government build and retain AI-literate staff across all 16 ministries and satellite offices in provinces and municipalities (Chapter 14)?

Estonia did it with 1.3 million people. Singapore did it as a city-state. The UAE did it by creating a ministry. BARMM can do it with a Bangsamoro Executive Order on AI-Augmented Governance — a policy instrument that the Chief Minister can issue, that BPDA can implement, and that Parliament can oversee.

The Thread That Connects Everything

I have spent my career at the intersection of governance, technology, and development in the Bangsamoro region. I drafted the Bangsamoro Local Governance Code at MILG. I managed the AHME Scholarship Program at MBHTE. I built legislative research tools for Parliament. I founded MoroTech and MoroAcademy to extend technology and training beyond government walls. This guidebook sits at that same intersection.

It is not a technology manual — it is a governance guidebook that uses technology as a force multiplier. It is not a policy paper — it is a practical handbook that you can use tomorrow. It is not a vision document — it is a collection of workflows, prompts, and protocols that work today, with tools that are available right now, at costs that range from free to modest.

The Bangsamoro Autonomous Region represents one of the most ambitious self-governance projects in Southeast Asia. The Bangsamoro Organic Law created a framework. The BTA built the institutions. The first regular government will test whether those institutions can deliver for the people they serve.

AI does not determine whether that test succeeds or fails. The people working inside those institutions determine that. AI is the tool that gives those people more time to think, more capacity to analyze, more speed to deliver, and more rigor to verify.

Use it.

Notes

[^1]: Smart Nation and Digital Government Office, National AI Strategy 2.0 (Singapore: Prime Minister’s Office, December 2023).

[^2]: Smart Nation Singapore, “Ask Jamie Virtual Assistant” and “OneService App,” accessed March 2026, https://www.smartnation.gov.sg.

[^3]: UAE Government, “Appointment of Minister of State for Artificial Intelligence,” Official Gazette, October 2017.

[^4]: UAE National Program for Artificial Intelligence, UAE Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (Abu Dhabi: UAE Government, 2023 update).

[^5]: Digital Dubai, “DubaiNow: Smart Services Platform,” accessed March 2026, https://www.digitaldubai.ae.

[^6]: e-Estonia, “e-Governance Fact Sheet,” accessed March 2026, https://e-estonia.com. BARMM’s population per Philippine Statistics Authority, 2020 Census of Population and Housing.

[^7]: Republic of Estonia Government CIO Office, Kratt: AI Strategy for Government Services (Tallinn: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications, 2019).

[^8]: Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority, 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028, Chapter 8: Harnessing Technology and Innovations (Cotabato City: BPDA, 2023).

[^9]: BDP Chapter 8 identifies six flagship programs under science and technology, focusing on research capacity, technology transfer, and innovation ecosystems. It does not include digital governance platforms, AI policy, or public administration technology as program areas. See BDP 2023-2028, pp. 201-218.

[^10]: The e-Bangsamoro platform is under active development by the author. Architecture: React 19 + Django 6.0 + PostgreSQL 18, four-portal design (Parliamentary, Ministerial, Budget, Citizen).

[^11]: BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 8, Section 8.3 (ICT Infrastructure Development). The plan acknowledges limited broadband penetration and uneven connectivity across BARMM provinces, particularly in island municipalities of Sulu, Tawi-Tawi, and Basilan.

[^12]: Republic Act No. 11054, “Bangsamoro Organic Law,” Article XVI, Section 1 (Transition Period).

[^13]: Republic Act No. 11054, Article XVI, Section 2 (First Regular Elections). The BOL mandated elections upon the expiration of the transition period; subsequent legislation (Republic Act No. 11593) synchronized the BARMM elections with the 2025 national elections.

[^14]: As of March 2026, the BTA Parliament has enacted 89 Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts. See the legislative index maintained in this guidebook’s companion repository.

[^15]: See “About the Author” for the author’s full governance, technology, and development background.

Back to Table of Contents
Glossary

Glossary of AI and Digital Governance Terms

This glossary defines terms used throughout this guidebook. Definitions are written in plain language for government staff who may be encountering these concepts for the first time. Terms are organized alphabetically.

ADDRESS IT — A seven-step methodology for Complete Staff Work (CSW) used across BARMM offices. The acronym stands for: Analyze context, Define/Deliberate the problem, Develop options, Research, Explore options, Submit, Sustain implementation, and Iterate. See Appendix E for the AI-enhanced version.

Adl (Justice) — An Islamic principle requiring fairness and equity. In the AI context, Adl demands that AI-assisted decisions do not create or reinforce bias against any group. See Chapter 12, Section 12.2.2.

AI (Artificial Intelligence) — Computer systems that perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence. In government work, AI typically means tools that read, write, summarize, translate, and analyze text. This guidebook focuses on generative AI tools that produce text outputs from text inputs.

AI Agent — An AI system that can perform multi-step tasks autonomously, breaking down complex requests into subtasks, using tools, and making intermediate decisions. More capable than a single-prompt interaction. Government applications include automated document routing and legislative research assistants.

AI Ethics Framework — A set of principles and rules governing how an organization uses AI. Chapter 12 proposes a Bangsamoro-specific framework grounded in moral governance and Islamic values. See also: Amanah, Adl, Maslahah, Shura.

AI Hallucination — When an AI tool generates text that sounds correct but is factually wrong. Examples include citing a BAA number that does not exist, attributing a provision to the wrong article, or inventing a statistic. Chapter 3’s “Trust but Verify” protocol addresses this risk.

AI Literacy — The ability to understand what AI can and cannot do, write effective prompts, evaluate AI outputs critically, and integrate AI tools into professional workflows. Chapter 2 provides the foundation. Chapter 14 covers training programs.

AI Strategy — A formal government document that assigns institutional ownership, training mandates, ethics standards, procurement policies, and performance indicators for AI adoption. Section 15.4 calls for a Bangsamoro AI Strategy.

Algorithm — A set of step-by-step instructions that a computer follows to complete a task. AI tools use algorithms to process text, recognize patterns, and generate outputs.

Amanah (Trustworthiness) — An Islamic principle requiring honesty and reliability. In the AI context, Amanah demands that AI-assisted outputs are verified for accuracy before submission. See Chapter 12, Section 12.2.1.

API (Application Programming Interface) — A set of rules that allows one software application to communicate with another. APIs enable AI tools to connect with government platforms like e-Bangsamoro.

Automation — Using technology to perform tasks without continuous human intervention. In government, automation ranges from auto-routing citizen complaints to generating standard reports. AI adds intelligence to automation by handling unstructured tasks like summarization and analysis.

BAA (Bangsamoro Autonomy Act) — Legislation enacted by the Bangsamoro Parliament. As of March 2026, the BTA Parliament has enacted 89 BAAs covering administrative structure, civil service, education, public finance, and other governance domains.

BARMM (Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao) — The autonomous political entity in the southern Philippines created by the Bangsamoro Organic Law (Republic Act No. 11054). BARMM replaced the Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (ARMM) in 2019.

BDP (Bangsamoro Development Plan) — The medium-term development framework for BARMM. The 2nd BDP covers 2023-2028 and contains six goals, eight strategies, and quantified socioeconomic targets. Referenced throughout this guidebook, especially Chapter 5.

Bias — Systematic errors in AI outputs that favor or disfavor certain groups, perspectives, or outcomes. Bias can enter through training data, prompt design, or the absence of local context. Chapter 12 addresses bias in the context of Islamic values and moral governance.

BOL (Bangsamoro Organic Law) — Republic Act No. 11054, the foundational law that created BARMM and established its governance structure. The BOL defines Parliament’s powers, the Chief Minister’s authority, the fiscal framework, and the rights of the Bangsamoro people.

BPDA (Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority) — The BARMM ministry responsible for development planning, coordination, and monitoring. BPDA manages the BDP and coordinates with national planning agencies.

BTA (Bangsamoro Transition Authority) — The transitional government established under BOL Article XVI to govern BARMM until the first regular elections. The BTA exercises both legislative and executive powers.

Budget Portal — One of e-Bangsamoro’s four portals. Handles budget preparation, authorization tracking, execution monitoring, and COA audit integration. See Section 15.2.

Chain of Thought — A prompting technique where you ask the AI to show its reasoning step by step before giving a final answer. Useful for complex analytical tasks like legal analysis or budget comparison, because you can see where the reasoning goes wrong and correct it.

Chatbot — An AI application designed to simulate conversation with users. Government chatbots handle citizen inquiries, route service requests, and provide information. Singapore’s Ask Jamie is an example.

Citizen Portal — One of e-Bangsamoro’s four portals. Provides public access to government services, transparency dashboards, document repositories, and feedback mechanisms. See Section 15.2.

Claude — An AI assistant developed by Anthropic. Claude is one of the primary AI tools referenced in this guidebook for text generation, document analysis, research synthesis, and workflow automation.

Cloud Computing — Delivering computing services (storage, processing, software) over the internet rather than from local hardware. Most AI tools run on cloud infrastructure. Government use requires awareness of where data is stored and processed.

COA (Commission on Audit) — The constitutional body that audits all government agencies in the Philippines, including BARMM. COA annual audit reports are the primary source material for parliamentary oversight. See Chapter 10.

Codification — The process of organizing, harmonizing, and consolidating enacted legislation into a systematic legal code. Chapter 11 covers AI-assisted codification of the 89 BAAs.

Complete Staff Work (CSW) — A methodology requiring that any document submitted to a decision-maker is complete, accurate, and ready for signature. BARMM uses the ADDRESS IT framework for CSW. See Appendix E.

Context Window — The maximum amount of text an AI tool can process in a single conversation. Larger context windows allow you to upload longer documents. As of 2026, leading AI tools support context windows ranging from 128,000 to 1,000,000 tokens.

Cross-Referencing — Comparing provisions across multiple laws, policies, or documents to identify overlaps, conflicts, or gaps. AI excels at cross-referencing large document sets. See Chapter 7, Section 7.2.3.

Dashboard — A visual display that presents key data and performance indicators in a single view. AI-assisted dashboards automate data aggregation and visualization. See Chapter 9.

Data Classification — The process of categorizing government data by sensitivity level (public, internal, confidential, restricted). Determines which documents can be uploaded to AI tools and which require additional safeguards. See Chapter 4.

Data Privacy — The protection of personal and sensitive information from unauthorized access, use, or disclosure. Chapter 4 covers data classification and privacy safeguards for AI use in government. The Data Privacy Act of 2012 (Republic Act No. 10173) governs data protection in the Philippines.

Digital Governance — The use of digital technologies to improve government operations, service delivery, transparency, and citizen engagement. This guidebook treats AI as one component of a broader digital governance strategy.

Digital Literacy — The ability to use digital tools and technologies effectively. AI literacy (defined separately) is a subset of digital literacy focused specifically on AI tools and workflows.

Digital Transformation — The process of fundamentally changing how an organization operates by integrating digital technology into all areas of work. Goes beyond digitizing paper forms to redesigning workflows around technology capabilities.

e-Bangsamoro — A four-portal e-governance platform under development for the Bangsamoro government. Portals: Parliamentary, Ministerial, Budget, and Citizen. See Section 15.2.

e-Governance — The use of information and communication technology (ICT) to deliver government services, exchange information, and enable transactions between government and citizens, businesses, and other government agencies.

Embedding — A mathematical representation of text that captures its meaning as numbers. AI systems use embeddings to understand how words and documents relate to each other. Enables semantic search — finding documents by meaning rather than exact keyword matches.

Ethics — The moral principles governing the use of AI in government. Chapter 12 grounds AI ethics in both secular governance principles (moral governance, transparency, accountability) and Islamic values (Amanah, Adl, Shura, Maslahah).

Explainability — The ability of an AI system to describe how it reached a particular output or recommendation. In government, explainability matters because decisions must be justifiable to oversight bodies and the public. If you cannot explain why the AI produced a result, you should not rely on that result for official action.

Few-Shot Prompting — A prompting technique where you include two or three examples of the desired output format in your prompt. The AI uses these examples as a template. Effective for standardizing output format across different tasks.

Fine-Tuning — The process of training an existing AI model on a specific dataset to improve its performance for a particular domain. Fine-tuning a model on BARMM legislation would make it more accurate on Bangsamoro legal questions. Requires technical expertise and computing resources.

GAA (General Appropriations Act) — The annual law that authorizes government spending for a fiscal year. In BARMM, the GAA is enacted by Parliament based on the Chief Minister’s proposed budget. See Chapter 6.

Gemini — An AI model developed by Google. Available through Google’s AI tools and integrated into NotebookLM. One of the AI platforms referenced in this guidebook.

Generative AI — AI systems that create new content — text, images, code, audio — based on patterns learned from training data. This guidebook focuses on generative AI for text, which is the primary modality for government document work.

GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) — A type of AI architecture that processes text by predicting the next word in a sequence. The “Transformer” refers to the underlying neural network design. GPT models power several commercial AI tools.

Guardrails — Rules and limitations built into AI systems or organizational policies to prevent harmful, inaccurate, or inappropriate outputs. Chapter 12 discusses guardrails for BARMM AI use.

Human-in-the-Loop — A design principle where AI systems require human review and approval before their outputs are acted upon. Every AI workflow in this guidebook follows this principle. The AI drafts; the human decides. See Chapter 3’s Four-Step Workflow.

ICT (Information and Communications Technology) — The broader category of digital tools including hardware, software, networks, and telecommunications. AI is a subset of ICT capabilities.

Interoperability — The ability of different systems, platforms, or databases to exchange and use data. Interoperability between e-Bangsamoro’s portals, BangsamoroHR, and other BARMM platforms is what enables AI to connect data across government functions. Estonia’s X-Road is a model for interoperability.

IRR (Implementing Rules and Regulations) — The detailed rules issued by the executive branch to operationalize an enacted law. BAAs require IRRs to specify implementation procedures. Chapter 11 covers AI-assisted IRR development.

Institutional Memory — The collective knowledge, experiences, precedents, and informal practices accumulated within an organization over time. Chapter 10 describes how AI-searchable knowledge bases preserve institutional memory across leadership transitions.

Knowledge Base — A structured collection of documents, data, and reference materials that AI tools can search and reference. Chapter 4 covers building an institutional knowledge base for BARMM. NotebookLM (Appendix B) is one tool for creating knowledge bases.

Large Language Model (LLM) — An AI system trained on vast amounts of text data that can understand and generate human language. Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini are LLMs. The “large” refers to billions of parameters (numerical values) that the model uses to process text.

Machine Learning (ML) — A subset of AI where systems improve their performance by learning from data without being explicitly programmed for every scenario. LLMs are one application of machine learning.

Maslahah (Public Interest) — An Islamic jurisprudential principle that evaluates actions based on whether they serve the public welfare. In the AI context, Maslahah is the test for whether an AI application should be adopted: does it genuinely serve the Bangsamoro people’s interests? See Chapter 12, Section 12.2.4.

Maturity Model — A framework that describes levels of organizational capability. Chapter 13 defines three AI maturity levels for BARMM: Level 1 (Individual Use), Level 2 (Team Integration), Level 3 (Institutional Adoption).

Ministerial Portal — One of e-Bangsamoro’s four portals. Supports program management, implementation tracking, reporting, and procurement documentation. See Section 15.2.

MOA (Memorandum of Agreement) — A formal document between two or more parties establishing a cooperative arrangement. In BARMM, MOAs govern intergovernmental relationships, partnership programs, and resource-sharing arrangements.

Model — In AI terminology, the trained software system that processes inputs and generates outputs. When this guidebook says “the AI model,” it refers to the underlying system (Claude, GPT, Gemini) that powers the tool you use.

MOP (Manual of Operations) — A document that defines the standard operating procedures, organizational structure, and workflow processes for a government office. AI can assist in drafting and maintaining MOPs.

Moral Governance — A BARMM governance principle emphasizing ethical conduct, transparency, accountability, and values-based leadership. The BTA’s 12 Pillars of Moral Governance inform the AI ethics framework in Chapter 12.

MOST (Ministry of Science and Technology) — The BARMM ministry responsible for science, technology, and innovation policy. A potential institutional owner for BARMM’s AI strategy (see Section 15.1).

Multimodal AI — AI systems that process multiple types of input — text, images, audio, video — rather than text alone. Multimodal capabilities allow you to upload a scanned document image and have the AI extract its text, or provide a chart and have the AI describe its contents.

Natural Language Processing (NLP) — The branch of AI that deals with the interaction between computers and human language. NLP enables AI tools to understand prompts, extract meaning from documents, translate between languages, and generate text.

NotebookLM — A Google AI tool that allows users to upload documents and have AI-powered conversations with them. Useful for building topic-specific knowledge bases. See Appendix B.

Obsidian — A knowledge management application that stores notes as local markdown files with bidirectional linking. Used in this guidebook’s author’s toolkit as a persistent knowledge base. See Appendix B.

OCR (Optical Character Recognition) — Technology that converts scanned images of text (such as PDF scans of printed documents) into machine-readable text. Essential for making older BARMM documents accessible to AI analysis.

Open Data — Government data that is freely available to the public in machine-readable formats. Open data enables transparency, accountability, and citizen participation. See Chapter 10, Section 10.3.

Open Source — Software whose source code is publicly available and can be freely used, modified, and distributed. Several AI tools and frameworks are open source.

Output — The text, table, analysis, or other content that an AI tool generates in response to a prompt. All AI outputs require human review before use in official government documents.

Parameters — The numerical values inside an AI model that determine how it processes text. More parameters generally means more capability. Current LLMs have billions to trillions of parameters. You do not need to understand parameters to use AI effectively.

Parliamentary Portal — One of e-Bangsamoro’s four portals. Supports legislation tracking, bill drafting workflows, committee management, and plenary session records. See Section 15.2.

PPA (Programs, Projects, and Activities) — The standard classification for government interventions in Philippine development planning. PPAs are the building blocks of the BDP and annual work plans. AI helps align PPAs with strategic goals and track their implementation.

Prompt — The text instruction you give to an AI tool. A prompt tells the AI what role to play, what context to consider, what task to perform, and what format to use for the output. Chapters 2 and 3 teach structured prompting for government work.

Prompt Engineering — The skill of writing effective prompts that produce accurate, useful AI outputs. Includes techniques like role assignment, context supply, format specification, and iterative refinement. Chapter 3 covers this as the “Four-Step AI Workflow.”

Prompt Library — A maintained collection of tested, reusable prompts for common tasks. Appendix A provides a prompt library organized by governance function. Teams at Level 2 maturity share prompt libraries to ensure consistency.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — A technique where AI retrieves relevant information from a specific document set before generating its response. RAG reduces hallucination by grounding AI outputs in actual source material. NotebookLM uses a form of RAG.

Resolution — A formal expression of the opinion, will, or decision of the Bangsamoro Parliament. Unlike BAAs, resolutions do not have the force and effect of law. The BTA has filed over 570 resolutions.

Role Prompting — A technique where you tell the AI to assume a specific professional role (e.g., “You are a parliamentary oversight analyst for the Bangsamoro Parliament”). Role prompting improves output relevance and tone. Used extensively throughout this guidebook.

Rule of Signature — A principle stated in this guidebook: if you would not sign a document without reading it, do not submit an AI-generated document without reviewing it. The person who signs bears accountability, regardless of how the document was produced.

Semantic Search — Searching for documents or information based on meaning rather than exact keyword matches. AI-powered semantic search finds relevant documents even when they use different terminology than your query.

Shari’ah — Islamic law derived from the Quran, Sunnah, and scholarly consensus. The BOL requires that legislation and governance in BARMM be consistent with Shari’ah principles. Chapter 12 addresses Shari’ah considerations for AI adoption.

Shura (Consultation) — An Islamic principle requiring collective deliberation before making significant decisions. In the AI context, Shura demands stakeholder consultation before deploying AI in government processes that affect communities. See Chapter 12, Section 12.2.3.

SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) — A documented set of step-by-step instructions for carrying out routine operations. At Level 2 and Level 3 maturity, SOPs incorporate AI-assisted steps with defined human review checkpoints.

Structured Output — AI output that follows a specific format — tables, numbered lists, labeled sections, JSON — rather than free-form text. Requesting structured output makes AI responses more consistent and easier to integrate into government documents.

System Prompt — A set of instructions given to an AI tool that governs its behavior throughout a conversation. System prompts can establish the AI’s role, define constraints, and specify output formats. Advanced AI tools allow custom system prompts.

Template — A pre-structured document format that standardizes outputs. This guidebook provides templates for legislative briefers (Appendix F), budget briefers (Appendix G), and the 90-Day AI Adoption Plan (Appendix H).

Token — The basic unit of text that an AI model processes. A token is roughly three-quarters of a word in English. Token limits determine how much text you can input and how long an output the AI can generate. When this guidebook refers to “context window,” it means the total number of tokens available.

Training Data — The text corpus that an AI model learned from during its development. Training data determines what the model “knows.” AI tools trained primarily on English-language text may perform less well on Filipino or Arabic content.

Transformer — The neural network architecture underlying modern LLMs. Transformers process text by calculating relationships between all words in a passage simultaneously, which enables them to understand context and generate coherent text.

Transparency — In AI governance, the principle that government should disclose when AI tools are used in producing official documents, analysis, or decisions. Chapter 12 proposes a BARMM transparency standard.

Trust but Verify — The core protocol for using AI outputs in government work, introduced in Chapter 3. Accept AI outputs as useful starting points but verify every factual claim, citation, number, and legal reference against authoritative sources before submitting.

Vendor Lock-In — A situation where an organization becomes dependent on a single AI provider and cannot switch without significant cost or disruption. Chapter 12’s integrity pillar warns against vendor lock-in that compromises government independence.

Verification — The process of checking AI outputs against authoritative sources to confirm accuracy. Verification is the non-negotiable step in every AI workflow described in this guidebook. Without verification, AI outputs are drafts, not documents.

Wali — The ceremonial head of the Bangsamoro government, representing the moral authority of the Bangsamoro people. The office is established under BOL Article VIII, Section 2. Referenced in Chapter 12’s discussion of moral governance.

Workflow — A defined sequence of steps for completing a task. This guidebook provides AI-augmented workflows for planning (Ch 5), budgeting (Ch 6), legislation (Ch 7), implementation (Ch 8), M&E (Ch 9), oversight (Ch 10), and codification (Ch 11).

X-Road — Estonia’s data exchange platform that connects all government databases. Referenced in Section 15.1 as a model for BARMM’s digital governance infrastructure.

Zero-Shot Prompting — A prompting technique where you ask the AI to perform a task without providing examples. The AI relies entirely on its training data and your instructions. Simpler than few-shot prompting but may produce less consistent formatting.

Back to Table of Contents

Appendices

Appendix A — Prompt Library for Common Government Tasks

This appendix provides ready-to-use prompts for six governance domains. Each prompt follows the structure taught in Chapters 2 and 3: role, context, task, format, constraints. Copy the prompt, replace the bracketed placeholders with your specifics, and review the output against the quality check before using it.

How to use this library: Find your governance domain (A.1 through A.6). Pick the prompt closest to your task. Modify the bracketed items. Run the prompt. Verify the output against the quality check listed with each prompt.

A.1 Planning and Strategy Prompts

Prompt A.1.1 — BDP Alignment Check

Task: Verify whether a proposed program aligns with BDP 2023-2028 goals.

You are a planning officer for a BARMM ministry.

I am preparing a program proposal for [PROGRAM NAME]. The program aims to [BRIEF DESCRIPTION, 2-3 sentences].

Review this program against the 2nd Bangsamoro Development Plan (BDP 2023-2028) and identify:
1. Which of the 6 BDP goals this program supports (list goal number and name)
2. Which specific BDP strategies it addresses
3. Any BDP macroeconomic or sectoral targets it contributes to
4. Gaps — BDP goals or strategies this program does NOT address but could

Format your response as a table with columns: BDP Goal, Strategy, Target, Alignment Evidence, Gap/Opportunity.

Expected output: Alignment matrix table with 4-6 rows. Quality check: Verify BDP goal numbers and strategy names against the actual BDP 2023-2028 document.

Prompt A.1.2 — Situational Analysis Organizer

Task: Structure raw data into a SWOT framework for an office strategic plan.

You are a planning analyst for [OFFICE NAME] in BARMM.

I will provide raw data from stakeholder consultations, performance reports, and environmental scans. Organize this data into a SWOT analysis following these rules:
- Strengths and Weaknesses are INTERNAL (within the office's control)
- Opportunities and Threats are EXTERNAL (in the environment)
- Each item must be specific and evidence-based, not generic
- Limit to 5-7 items per quadrant
- For each item, note the source (consultation, report, or scan)

Here is the raw data:
[PASTE YOUR DATA]

Expected output: Four-quadrant SWOT table with sourced entries. Quality check: Confirm internal/external classification is correct. Remove any generic items (“limited budget” without specifics).

Prompt A.1.3 — Theory of Change Narrative

Task: Draft a theory of change statement for a program proposal.

You are a development planning specialist for BARMM.

Draft a Theory of Change narrative for the following program:
- Program: [NAME]
- Problem statement: [WHAT PROBLEM DOES IT ADDRESS]
- Target beneficiaries: [WHO]
- Key interventions: [WHAT THE PROGRAM WILL DO]
- Expected outcomes: [WHAT CHANGES SHOULD HAPPEN]

Structure the narrative as:
1. If-Then chain: "If [intervention], then [output], leading to [outcome], contributing to [impact]"
2. Assumptions: What must be true for each link in the chain to hold
3. Risks: What could break the chain
4. Evidence base: What evidence supports each causal link

Keep it under 500 words. Write in active voice, present tense.

Expected output: Theory of change narrative with explicit causal logic. Quality check: Test each “if-then” link. Does the logic hold? Are assumptions realistic for BARMM conditions?

Prompt A.1.4 — Development Indicator Formulation

Task: Convert a vague objective into measurable indicators.

I am preparing the Results Framework for [OFFICE/PROGRAM NAME].

Convert the following objective into SMART indicators:
Objective: "[PASTE YOUR OBJECTIVE]"

For each indicator, provide:
- Indicator statement (specific, measurable)
- Baseline value (or note if baseline data collection is needed)
- Target value with timeline
- Data source
- Collection frequency
- Responsible office

Generate 2-3 indicators per objective: at least one output indicator and one outcome indicator.

Expected output: Results framework table with 2-3 indicators. Quality check: Each indicator must be independently verifiable. Remove any indicator you cannot actually measure with available data.

Prompt A.1.5 — Stakeholder Mapping

Task: Identify and classify stakeholders for a new program or policy.

You are a planning officer preparing a stakeholder analysis for [PROGRAM/POLICY NAME] in BARMM.

Map all relevant stakeholders using this framework:
- Classify by influence (high/low) and interest (high/low)
- For each stakeholder, note: name/office, role in the program, engagement strategy, potential risk if not engaged
- Include: BARMM ministries, national government agencies, LGUs, CSOs, private sector, development partners, traditional/religious leaders, affected communities

Present as a table, then provide a brief engagement priority note (who to engage first and why).

Expected output: Stakeholder matrix with 10-15 entries. Quality check: Verify that no major stakeholder category is missing. Check that influence/interest classifications match reality.

Prompt A.1.6 — GAD Analysis for Program Design

Task: Integrate gender analysis into a program proposal.

Analyze [PROGRAM NAME] for gender responsiveness. Provide: (1) gender issues in the sector, (2) how the program addresses or fails to address them, (3) recommended adjustments, (4) sex-disaggregated indicators, (5) GAD budget approach. Reference RA 9710 and PCW Harmonized GAD Guidelines.

Program description: [BRIEF DESCRIPTION]

Expected output: GAD analysis with specific recommendations. Quality check: Confirm RA 9710 references are accurate. Ensure recommendations are actionable within BARMM context.

A.2 Budgeting and Finance Prompts

Prompt A.2.1 — Budget Variance Analysis

Task: Analyze year-over-year changes in a ministry’s budget.

You are a budget analyst for the Bangsamoro Parliament.

I will provide two years of budget data for [MINISTRY NAME]:
- FY [YEAR 1] GAA allocation: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE]
- FY [YEAR 2] proposed budget: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE]

Analyze:
1. Total budget change (amount and percentage)
2. Line-item changes: which programs increased, decreased, or are new
3. Top 3 largest increases and their justification (if stated)
4. Top 3 largest decreases and potential impact
5. Personnel Services vs. MOOE vs. Capital Outlay ratio comparison
6. Flags: any unusual patterns (e.g., >50% increase in a single line item, new programs without enabling legislation)

Present findings in a summary table followed by a narrative analysis.

Expected output: Variance analysis table and 300-500 word narrative. Quality check: Verify all dollar figures and percentages by manual calculation on the top 5 line items.

Prompt A.2.2 — Budget Scrutiny Questions

Task: Generate questions for committee budget deliberations.

You are a committee secretary preparing budget scrutiny questions for the Committee on [COMMITTEE NAME].

Based on the following ministry budget proposal:
[PASTE BUDGET SUMMARY OR KEY FIGURES]

Generate 10 scrutiny questions covering:
- Utilization rate of prior year's budget
- Justification for significant increases
- Alignment with BDP 2023-2028 priorities
- Personnel complement vs. filled positions
- Status of prior year's unfunded programs
- Capital outlay implementation readiness
- Revenue generation targets (if applicable)

For each question, note: the specific budget item it targets and the expected response format.

Expected output: Numbered list of 10 questions with budget item references. Quality check: Confirm each question references an actual line item in the budget proposal.

Prompt A.2.3 — COA Finding Response Drafter

Task: Draft management responses to audit observations.

You are an accountant preparing management comments for COA audit observations.

For each audit observation below, draft a management response that:
1. Acknowledges the finding (do not be defensive)
2. Explains the context (why the situation occurred)
3. States the corrective action taken or planned
4. Provides the timeline for completion
5. Names the responsible officer

Observation: [PASTE COA OBSERVATION]

Write in formal but clear language. Each response should be 100-200 words.

Expected output: Structured management response per observation. Quality check: Ensure corrective actions are specific and have realistic timelines. Have the accountant verify technical accuracy.

Prompt A.2.4 — Obligation and Disbursement Tracker Summary

Task: Summarize financial performance for a quarterly report.

Analyze the following obligation and disbursement data for [OFFICE NAME], Q[QUARTER] FY [YEAR]:

[PASTE DATA: allotment, obligations, disbursements by expense class]

Provide:
1. Obligation rate by expense class (PS, MOOE, CO) and total
2. Disbursement rate by expense class and total
3. Comparison to same quarter previous year (if data provided)
4. Programs with lowest obligation rates and potential causes
5. Recommendations to improve utilization before year-end

Format as a dashboard summary: table first, then 3-5 bullet points of key findings.

Expected output: Financial dashboard summary. Quality check: Recalculate rates manually for the three largest line items.

Prompt A.2.5 — Annual Procurement Plan AI Review

Task: Check an APP for completeness and compliance.

Review the following Annual Procurement Plan for compliance with:
- RA 9184 (Government Procurement Reform Act) requirements
- BAA 84 (Bangsamoro Budget System Act) provisions on procurement planning
- DBM-GPPB Joint Circular guidelines

Check for:
1. Completeness of required columns (PMO, mode of procurement, schedule, ABC)
2. Procurement modes selected — are they appropriate for the ABC amounts?
3. Items that may require early procurement action based on lead times
4. Any items that appear to duplicate existing contracts
5. Total ABC vs. approved budget — is the APP within budget limits?

[PASTE APP DATA OR KEY SECTIONS]

Expected output: Compliance review with specific findings. Quality check: Verify procurement thresholds against current RA 9184 IRR schedules.

Prompt A.2.6 — Budget Brief Talking Points

Task: Prepare talking points for a budget presentation to Parliament.

Prepare budget presentation talking points for [MINISTRY NAME] FY [YEAR] budget defense before the Committee on Appropriations.

Key data:
- Total proposed budget: [AMOUNT]
- Key programs: [LIST TOP 5]
- Major changes from prior year: [SUMMARY]

Structure as:
1. Opening statement (2-3 sentences): mandate, key achievement, budget request
2. Priority programs (3-5 bullets each): what it does, who benefits, budget amount, expected output
3. Anticipated questions and prepared responses (5 items)
4. Closing appeal (2-3 sentences)

Keep total under 800 words. Use specific numbers, not vague claims.

Expected output: Structured talking points document. Quality check: Verify all budget figures match the official proposal. Remove any claim not supported by actual data.

A.3 Legislative and Policy Prompts

Prompt A.3.1 — Policy Landscape Scan

Task: Map existing laws relevant to a proposed bill.

You are a legislative researcher for the Bangsamoro Parliament.

I am preparing a bill on [TOPIC]. Scan the following list of enacted Bangsamoro Autonomy Acts and identify every BAA that:
1. Directly addresses this topic
2. Contains provisions that may overlap or conflict
3. Establishes agencies, commissions, or offices with related mandates
4. Defines terms that this bill must adopt or reconcile

Also identify relevant national laws (Republic Acts) that apply to the same subject matter.

Present findings as a table: Law, Key Provisions, Relationship to Proposed Bill (supports/conflicts/supplements).

[PASTE BAA LIST OR PROVIDE TOPIC DETAILS]

Expected output: Legal landscape matrix. Quality check: Verify every BAA number and title cited. Cross-check against the official BAA index.

Prompt A.3.2 — Explanatory Note Drafter

Task: Draft an explanatory note for a filed bill.

You are a legislative staff member of the Bangsamoro Parliament.

Draft an explanatory note for [BILL TITLE/NUMBER] following this structure:
1. Context and rationale — what problem does this bill address?
2. Legal basis — BOL provisions, national laws, and existing BAAs that authorize this legislation
3. Objectives — what the bill aims to achieve
4. Key features — summary of major provisions (numbered)
5. Fiscal impact — estimated cost and funding source
6. Expected beneficiaries — who benefits and how

Background information:
[PASTE POLICY BRIEF, RESEARCH NOTES, OR BILL SUMMARY]

Write in formal legislative language. Keep under 1,500 words.

Expected output: Complete explanatory note draft. Quality check: Verify every BOL article and BAA cited. Confirm the bill’s provisions match the explanatory note’s claims.

Prompt A.3.3 — Interpellation Question Tree

Task: Prepare questions for interpellation of a bill sponsor.

You are a legislative researcher preparing interpellation material for [MP NAME], who will question the sponsor of [BILL TITLE/NUMBER].

Generate a question tree with:
1. Opening questions (scope and intent) — 3 questions
2. Technical questions (specific provisions) — 5 questions
3. Fiscal questions (budget and funding) — 3 questions
4. Implementation questions (who, when, how) — 3 questions
5. Follow-up branches: for each question, provide one follow-up if the sponsor answers "yes" and one if they answer "no"

Base questions on: [PASTE BILL SUMMARY OR KEY PROVISIONS]

Write questions in parliamentary language. Each question should target a specific section or provision.

Expected output: Branching question tree with 14+ questions. Quality check: Ensure each question references a real provision in the bill.

Prompt A.3.4 — Committee Report Structure

Task: Draft the structure and key sections of a committee report.

Draft a committee report for [BILL TITLE/NUMBER] as reviewed by the Committee on [COMMITTEE NAME].

Include:
1. Background and referral history
2. Summary of committee hearings (dates, resource persons, key testimony)
3. Key issues raised during deliberations
4. Committee amendments (if any) with justification
5. Committee recommendation (approval, approval with amendments, or further study)

Source information:
[PASTE HEARING NOTES, TESTIMONY SUMMARIES, OR COMMITTEE DISCUSSION POINTS]

Follow the format prescribed by the BTA Rules of Procedure and the Bill Drafting Guidebook.

Expected output: Committee report draft. Quality check: Verify hearing dates and resource person names against actual records.

Prompt A.3.5 — Policy Recommendation Memo

Task: Draft a policy recommendation for executive action.

Draft a policy recommendation memo for [DECISION-MAKER TITLE AND NAME].

Subject: [POLICY ISSUE]

Structure:
1. Issue statement (1 paragraph)
2. Background and context (2-3 paragraphs)
3. Options analysis (3 options minimum):
   - Description of each option
   - Pros and cons
   - Fiscal implications
   - Legal considerations
   - Implementation feasibility
4. Recommended option with justification
5. Proposed next steps with timeline

Base the analysis on: [PASTE RESEARCH DATA, STAKEHOLDER INPUT, OR PRIOR MEMOS]

Expected output: Decision-ready policy memo. Quality check: Ensure the recommended option is genuinely the strongest, not just the first one listed. Verify fiscal figures.

Prompt A.3.6 — Amendment Language Drafter

Task: Draft specific amendment text for a bill under deliberation.

Draft amendment language for [BILL TITLE/NUMBER].

Amendment target: Section [NUMBER], which currently reads:
"[PASTE CURRENT TEXT]"

Proposed change: [DESCRIBE WHAT THE AMENDMENT SHOULD DO]

Provide:
1. The amended text (showing deletions in strikethrough and additions in bold, or using "DELETE... INSERT..." format)
2. Brief justification (2-3 sentences)
3. Impact on other sections of the bill (cross-reference check)
4. Potential objections the sponsor may raise

Use formal legislative drafting conventions per the Bill Drafting Guidebook.

Expected output: Amendment text with justification and impact analysis. Quality check: Verify the current text matches the actual bill. Check that the amendment does not create internal contradictions.

Prompt A.3.7 — IRR Framework Generator

Task: Create the skeletal framework for implementing rules and regulations.

Generate an IRR framework for [BAA NUMBER AND TITLE].

For each section of the law that requires implementing rules, provide:
1. The BAA section requiring IRR provisions
2. What the IRR must specify (rules, procedures, standards, timelines)
3. Which agency or office should lead drafting
4. Stakeholders who should be consulted
5. Timeline recommendation

Organize by chapter/article of the BAA. Flag any provisions that are self-executing and do not require IRR.

[PASTE BAA TEXT OR SUMMARY]

Expected output: IRR development roadmap table. Quality check: Verify every section reference against the actual BAA. Confirm the implementing agencies exist.

A.4 Administrative and Correspondence Prompts

Prompt A.4.1 — Executive Order Drafter

Task: Draft an executive order or administrative order.

Draft an [Executive Order / Administrative Order] for [ISSUING AUTHORITY].

Subject: [TOPIC]
Purpose: [WHAT IT ESTABLISHES, DIRECTS, OR AUTHORIZES]
Legal basis: [ENABLING LAW OR AUTHORITY]

Structure per standard BARMM format:
1. WHEREAS clauses (3-5, establishing context and authority)
2. NOW, THEREFORE clause
3. Numbered sections (purpose, scope, definition of terms, substantive provisions, effectivity)
4. Signature block with title and date

Substantive content: [PASTE YOUR NOTES OR POLICY DECISIONS]

Expected output: Complete EO/AO draft. Quality check: Verify the legal basis is valid. Confirm the issuing authority has jurisdiction.

Prompt A.4.2 — Official Correspondence Drafter

Task: Draft a formal letter from a BARMM official.

Draft an official letter from [SENDER NAME, TITLE, OFFICE] to [RECIPIENT NAME, TITLE, OFFICE].

Purpose: [REQUEST/ENDORSEMENT/REPLY/TRANSMITTAL/INVITATION]

Key points to cover:
[LIST THE SUBSTANTIVE CONTENT]

Requirements:
- Formal Philippine government correspondence format
- Opening salutation appropriate to the recipient's rank
- Clear action requested or information conveyed
- Appropriate closing and complimentary close
- Reference number placeholder: [REF NO.]

Keep under 400 words. Direct and respectful tone.

Expected output: Formal letter draft. Quality check: Verify recipient’s correct title and office name. Ensure the action requested is clear.

Prompt A.4.3 — Meeting Minutes Organizer

Task: Convert raw meeting notes into structured minutes.

Convert the following raw meeting notes into formal minutes:

[PASTE RAW NOTES]

Format:
1. Header: meeting title, date, time, venue, presiding officer
2. Attendees (with titles and offices)
3. Agenda items (numbered)
4. For each agenda item: summary of discussion, decisions made, action items (who, what, when)
5. Next meeting date
6. Prepared by / Noted by lines

Write in past tense, third person. Attribute decisions to the body, not individuals, unless a specific directive was given.

Expected output: Formatted meeting minutes. Quality check: Verify attendee names and titles. Confirm action items have specific responsible persons and deadlines.

Prompt A.4.4 — Travel/Activity Report

Task: Draft a post-activity report from field notes.

Draft an activity report based on the following field notes:

Activity: [NAME]
Date(s): [DATES]
Venue: [LOCATION]
Participants: [NUMBER AND TYPE]
Conducted by: [OFFICE/TEAM]

Raw notes:
[PASTE YOUR NOTES]

Structure:
1. Activity overview (1 paragraph)
2. Objectives
3. Proceedings/Activities conducted (chronological)
4. Key findings or outputs
5. Issues and challenges encountered
6. Recommendations
7. Annexes list (attendance sheet, photos, handouts)

Expected output: Complete activity report. Quality check: Verify dates, participant counts, and venue names.

Prompt A.4.5 — Memorandum of Agreement Framework

Task: Draft a framework MOA between two BARMM entities.

Draft a MOA between [PARTY A — name, office, represented by] and [PARTY B — name, office, represented by].
Purpose: [WHAT THE MOA ESTABLISHES]. Duration: [PERIOD].

Include: WHEREAS clauses, purpose, scope, responsibilities of each party, financial arrangements, confidentiality, amendment/termination, dispute resolution, effectivity, signature blocks.

Key terms: [PASTE KEY TERMS AND RESPONSIBILITIES]

Expected output: MOA framework draft. Quality check: Verify both parties have authority to enter the agreement. Ensure financial terms are specific.

A.5 Research and Reporting Prompts

Prompt A.5.1 — Literature Review Synthesizer

Task: Synthesize multiple sources into a structured review.

Synthesize the following sources into a literature review on [TOPIC]:

[PASTE SUMMARIES, EXCERPTS, OR SOURCE LIST]

Structure:
1. Overview of the research landscape (what has been studied, what gaps exist)
2. Key themes across sources (organize by theme, not by source)
3. Points of agreement across sources
4. Points of disagreement or debate
5. Implications for BARMM policy/governance
6. Research gaps that future work should address

Cite sources using [Author, Year] format. Keep to 1,500-2,000 words.

Expected output: Thematic literature review. Quality check: Verify that claims attributed to specific sources actually appear in those sources.

Prompt A.5.2 — Annual Report Section Drafter

Task: Draft a section of an office annual report from raw data.

Draft the [SECTION NAME] section of the FY [YEAR] Annual Report for [OFFICE NAME].

Data provided:
[PASTE KEY ACCOMPLISHMENTS, STATISTICS, NARRATIVES]

Structure:
1. Overview (2-3 sentences summarizing the year's performance)
2. Major accomplishments (bulleted, with specific numbers)
3. Key performance indicators achieved vs. targets (table format)
4. Challenges encountered
5. Plans for the coming year

Tone: factual, achievement-oriented, but honest about challenges. Write in past tense.
Use specific numbers and percentages, not vague qualifiers.

Expected output: Annual report section draft. Quality check: Verify every number against source data. Remove any unsupported superlatives.

Prompt A.5.3 — Position Paper Drafter

Task: Draft an office position paper on a policy issue.

Draft a position paper for [OFFICE NAME] on [POLICY ISSUE].

Office position: [SUMMARIZE THE OFFICE'S STANCE]

Structure:
1. Executive summary (200 words)
2. Background and context
3. Analysis of the issue (including stakeholder perspectives)
4. Office position and rationale
5. Supporting evidence
6. Recommendations
7. Conclusion

Sources and evidence: [PASTE RELEVANT DATA, LEGAL REFERENCES, PRECEDENTS]

Write in formal analytical tone. 2,000-2,500 words.

Expected output: Position paper draft. Quality check: Ensure the position is internally consistent. Verify all legal citations.

Prompt A.5.4 — Comparative Analysis Table Builder

Task: Build a comparison matrix across jurisdictions or programs.

Build a comparative analysis table for [TOPIC] across the following [jurisdictions/programs/offices]:
[LIST 3-5 ITEMS TO COMPARE]

Comparison dimensions:
[LIST 5-8 CRITERIA]

For each cell, provide: the specific practice or provision (not a vague summary), and note the source. Add a final row: "Key Takeaway for BARMM" — what BARMM can adopt or adapt from each.

Expected output: Structured comparison matrix. Quality check: Verify each cell’s content against the actual source.

A.6 M&E and Oversight Prompts

Prompt A.6.1 — KPI Dashboard Builder

Task: Create a performance monitoring dashboard from raw data.

Create a KPI dashboard for [OFFICE/PROGRAM NAME] based on the following data:

[PASTE PERFORMANCE DATA: targets, actual accomplishments, timelines]

For each KPI:
1. KPI name and definition
2. Target value
3. Actual value
4. Achievement rate (%)
5. Status: On Track (≥80%), At Risk (50-79%), Off Track (<50%)
6. Brief explanation for any Off Track or At Risk items

Present as a table. Then provide a 3-sentence executive summary highlighting: best performing KPI, worst performing KPI, and overall program health.

Expected output: KPI dashboard table with executive summary. Quality check: Recalculate achievement rates manually for the top 3 items.

Prompt A.6.2 — Program Evaluation Framework

Task: Design an evaluation framework for a BARMM program.

Design an evaluation framework for [PROGRAM NAME].

Program details:
- Duration: [START-END]
- Budget: [AMOUNT]
- Beneficiaries: [WHO AND HOW MANY]
- Key activities: [LIST]
- Expected outcomes: [LIST]

Include:
1. Evaluation questions (5-7, covering relevance, efficiency, effectiveness, impact, sustainability)
2. For each question: indicators, data sources, data collection methods, analysis approach
3. Evaluation timeline (when to conduct midterm vs. endline)
4. Stakeholder participation in the evaluation
5. Ethical considerations
6. Suggested evaluation team composition

Expected output: Evaluation framework document. Quality check: Ensure evaluation questions are answerable with available data. Confirm timeline is realistic.

Prompt A.6.3 — COA Compliance Self-Assessment

Task: Prepare a self-assessment against common COA findings.

Conduct a self-assessment for [OFFICE NAME] against the most common COA audit findings in BARMM:

Common finding categories:
1. Cash advances not liquidated within prescribed period
2. Procurement not following RA 9184 procedures
3. Fund utilization below 80% of allotment
4. Incomplete or missing supporting documents
5. Non-compliance with DBM budget execution guidelines
6. Payroll discrepancies
7. Property and inventory management gaps

For each category, help me document:
- Current office status (compliant/partially compliant/non-compliant)
- Evidence of compliance (what records exist)
- Gaps identified
- Corrective actions needed
- Timeline for corrective action

Expected output: Self-assessment checklist with corrective action plan. Quality check: Each compliance claim must be supported by documentary evidence.

Prompt A.6.4 — Oversight Analysis of Agency Performance

Task: Analyze an agency’s performance report for parliamentary oversight.

You are a legislative researcher analyzing [AGENCY NAME]'s performance for the Committee on [COMMITTEE NAME].

Performance data: [PASTE ANNUAL REPORT DATA OR KPIs]
Budget data: [PASTE BUDGET VS. UTILIZATION]

Analyze:
1. Overall mandate fulfillment: is the agency doing what its enabling law requires?
2. Budget utilization: how effectively is the budget being spent?
3. Output delivery: are committed outputs being delivered on time?
4. Gaps between legislative intent and actual implementation
5. Red flags: any patterns suggesting systemic problems

Generate 5 oversight questions for the committee chair to ask the agency head.

Expected output: Oversight analysis brief with questions. Quality check: Verify budget figures. Confirm the agency’s enabling law is correctly cited.

Prompt A.6.5 — Quarterly Monitoring Report Template

Task: Generate a standardized quarterly monitoring report.

Generate a quarterly monitoring report for [PROGRAM/PROJECT NAME], Q[QUARTER] FY [YEAR].

Data:
[PASTE: physical targets vs. accomplishments, financial data, timeline status]

Structure:
1. Executive summary (5 sentences)
2. Physical performance (table: target, actual, %, variance explanation)
3. Financial performance (table: allotment, obligation, disbursement, rates)
4. Implementation issues and bottlenecks
5. Corrective actions taken
6. Outlook for next quarter
7. Recommendations

Expected output: Complete quarterly monitoring report. Quality check: Verify all percentages by manual calculation. Confirm physical targets match the approved work plan.

Appendix B — Tool Setup Guides

These guides get you from zero to productive in one sitting. Each covers one tool. Screenshots will vary as interfaces update, but the logic stays the same.

B.1 Claude (Web and Desktop)
